### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/announcement/3rd-dasa-call-nomination

# 3rd Distinguished Alumni Service Award: Call for Nomination 2024
[Image: 3rd Distinguished Alumni Service Award: Call for Nomination 2024]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/)
**Wednesday, January 24, 2024**
## THE 3RD GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD (GC-DASA) FOR THE YEAR 2024
**GENERAL INFORMATION AND GUIDELINES**
Pursuant to Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. (GCAAI) Board Resolution No. 4, series of 2022, the GCAAI establishes the **DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD (DASA)** to honor Gordon College graduates who have excelled both in their professional and personal careers and who have served as an example to other alumni, students, and the community, both locally and globally. Starting this year, the GCAAI Board of Trustees will recognize the exemplary service and distinguished achievements that brought honor to the Institution and have made significant contributions to society. The GC-DASA recipients will exemplify the core values that the Institution uphold since its establishment: **Exceptional Character, Remarkable Excellence, And Unselfish Service.**
The GC-DASA shall be the highest honor bestowed by the Alumni Association and will be an event to be celebrated annually as part of the GC Founding Anniversary and/or GC Alumni Homecoming.
## GENERAL GUIDELINES:
1. Only graduates of Gordon College (or GC, formerly Olongapo City Colleges) are eligible for the GC-DASA. The nominee shall be a member of the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. as confirmed by its Board.
2. Any GC graduate, faculty member, or stakeholders may make nominations for the GC-DASA. Self–nominations are accepted. The nominee shall accomplish the Official Nomination Form to be provided by the GCAAI. No limitation will be placed on the total number of nominations accepted for consideration.
3. The nominee shall have no pending administrative or criminal case upon nomination. He/she shall exhibit a high degree of character & integrity.
4. Only one recipient per college/program will be awarded. In case the college/program will have no nominees/recipients, the awarding ceremony will still commence. Likewise, the GCAAI Board of Trustees can directly, without prejudice to the respective college, nominate and evaluate for such an award though the college/program have already a candidate.
5. Only living candidates are considered. In the event of the death of a candidate between notification of the award and the award ceremony, the award may be given posthumously with the permission of the family and the GCAAI. In the event that the awardee declines to accept the award and/or fails to attend the awarding ceremonies, the GCAAI reserves the right to withdraw the award.
6. A GC graduate sitting as a member of the selection committee or a member of the GCAAI Board of Trustees is not eligible to receive the award. This individual is eligible for nomination after a one-year waiting period from the date he/she step down from his/her role.
7. Recipients are only eligible for one GC-DASA in a lifetime.
8. The recipients will receive a plaque or certificate of recognition and will be posted on the official FB page of the GCAAI. It will announce the annual recipients through various methods and honor each recipient with the appropriate recognition.
9. If for any reason, the GCAAI is not able to conduct the Awards as planned (including due to tampering, unauthorized intervention, fraud, technical difficulties, pandemic, or any other causes beyond the control of the organizer which impact the administration, security, fairness, integrity or proper conduct of the Awards), then they reserve the right in their sole discretion to make any reasonable variation to the Awarding Ceremony.
10. The Nomination Committee and/or GCAAI Board of Trustees may disqualify any nominee that they reasonably determine has breached any of the terms and conditions or otherwise engaged in unlawful or other improper conduct that may bring the Awards into disrepute or jeopardize the fair and proper conduct of the Awards.
11. Other guidelines may be promulgated as decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees.
## NOMINATION COMMITTEE:
The Nomination Committee (NC) may be organized by the Dean/OIC in every college and/or the GCAAI. The committee must be composed of at least three (3) members from their respective colleges. The total count of committee members should be an odd number. The chairperson of the NC shall be the Dean or its duly authorized representative and for the GCAAI, any member can initiate such nomination. No member of the NC must be related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the nominees/candidates for awards. If the Dean/OIC is related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the candidates for awards, the Dean/OIC must inhibit him/herself from participating in the process. The approval shall come from the person next in rank.
The NC shall:
1. Abide by the criteria set forth by the GCAAI.
2. For nominees endorsed by the College, it shall issue a certificate of authenticity to all the documents that will be submitted to the GCAAI.
3. Deliberate on the qualifications of the nominees for the awards based on the criteria and the documents submitted.
4. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the result of nomination for approval.
5. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the resolution on any related issue that may arise from the results of the awards.
6. Ensure that the guidelines stipulated herein are followed.
## NOMINATION AND SELECTION PROCESS:
1. Complete the online/hard copy Nomination Form. This also forms part of the terms and conditions.
2. Nominators will receive an auto-generated copy via email of what they submitted, i.e., the nomination form including any attachments. In any case, it is not available, the NC may determine what means of notification it will conduct to the nominators to confirm the submission of the nomination form and its attachments.
3. Nominees and nominators will be contacted by the NC/GCAAI within two days of receipt of the nomination to acknowledge receipt.
4. A nominee may be asked to clarify or submit further information and evidence to assist the NC/GCAAI in assessing the nomination at any stage of the selection process.
5. The NC/GCAAI may also seek verification of the nominee’s achievements from their current or previous employer or other relevant individual or organization representative. By being nominated for this Award, the nominees are giving consent to the NC/GCAAI to contact these individuals to verify nominations.
6. Nominations will be reviewed and shortlisted by the NC/GCAAI and nominees who have not been shortlisted will be notified. The GC-DASA candidates will be asked to submit a brief profile for this purpose. The candidate shall garner a total score of 60 points to be included in the shortlist. If a nominee cannot meet the required points, he/she is automatically excluded from the shortlist.
7. Every member of the NC will recommend/endorse the final candidate to the GCAAI President and then, the GCAAI Board of Trustees, sitting as Awards Selection Committee, will issue a confirmation through a Board Resolution. The report on the results of the NC shall be signed by all members of the committee and certified by the chairperson.
8. In any case that such protest will be filed during the confirmation of the final candidates, the final decision on the protest shall be decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees. The GCAAI Board of Trustees’ decision is final on all matters relating to the nomination process and the selection of award recipients, and they are not required to provide feedback as to why a nomination was unsuccessful.
**CRITERIA:**
|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Indicators:** | **Evidence** | **Points** |
| Volunteer Works/Voluntary services within the Olongapo and Zambales Community  (25 points) | DocumentationPictures Certificate of Participation/ Recognition/Attendance,Certification from the Beneficiary or Organizer testifying the services rendered. by the nominee | 25 – complete evidence  15 – at least two pieces of evidence10 – one piece of evidence |
| Outstanding Performance from their respective Company/ Employer/ Profession  Affiliation/Membership to any accredited/legitimate organization certifying its outstanding status in the said organization.  (25 points) | Certification from their Employer/ Company/Accredited/Legitimate Organization attesting the Outstanding Performance of the nominee. Performance Evaluation Result with outstanding remarks certified by their HR or Employer | 25 – complete evidence  15 – one piece of evidence |
| Involvement in GC Events, Projects, and Community Outreach Programs (as a participant, contributor, or donor) (25 points) | Certificate of Recognition as Donor/Contributor given by their respective college; For Participants Certificate of participation or attendance Any Proof of donation/contribution (pictures or acknowledgment receipt), picture or documentation showing their attendance to the event/program | 25 – complete evidence  15 – one piece of evidence |
| Meritorious Award or Recognition given by their accredited professional organization, any award- giving body, employer, or industry associations/ federations.  (15 points) | Any proof of recognition/award (plaque, trophy, emblem, medal, certificate, etc.)Graduate milestones (Short Course Certificate, Master’s and Doctoral, Continuing Professional Development Certificates) Accomplishment related to professional growth, a new opportunity or achievement (such as landing a promotion, skill development, and so on.) | 15 – with complete evidence   10 – one piece of evidence0 – no evidence |
| Interview Result (10 points) | Results of the Interview conducted by the SC | Subject to the discretion of the SC |
| **TOTAL****POINTS** | | **100** |

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gordon-college-celebrates-world-teachers-day

# Gordon College Celebrates World Teachers’ Day
[Image: Gordon College Celebrates World Teachers’ Day]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, October 7, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE MAIN BLDG—The Office of the Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) hosted a World Teachers’ Day Celebration, together with various student organizations, which aims to honor the dedicated instructors of the institution on October 6, 2025, at Gordon College Main Bldg—Function Hall.
The event began with an opening prayer and remarks from Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, Gordon College President, and Dr. Darwin P. Paguio, Vice President for Academic Affairs.
“We cannot have a lavish lifestyle in teaching. But your dedication and passion will definitely [inaudible] through saying our students’ success— changing their lives, especially those who are underprivileged students, and you are making them a story,” said Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, showing her gratitude for the effort of Gordon College’s faculty.
Dr. Darwin P. Paguio also expressed to the faculty that the celebration isn’t just about their perseverance, but also to take care of all the hard work in the classrooms as they invest in nurturing curiosity that will nourish them for a lifetime.
After the opening ceremony, Performing Arts Students Ensemble (PASE) Voci tributed a chorale performance that honors the educators.
To spark joy with the event, the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Student Council, Midwifery Student Organization (MSO), and the Nursing Student Organization (NSO) hosted games to energize and entertain the teachers.
Furthermore, the PASE Ballo sparked warmth and embrace with a performance number.
Moreover, the Supreme Student Council (SSC) and Human Resource Management Unit (HRMU) conducted an awarding ceremony to appreciate the faculty’s skills with admiration and humor.
Meanwhile, Hon. Rolen Paulino Jr., City Mayor of Olongapo, held a speech and cash raffle for the faculty.
“Salamat sa sacrifices niyo, sa mahabang pasensiya ninyo, tiyaka sa lahat ng ginagawa niyo para sa mga students natin,” Paulino stated.
An Audio-Visual Presentation (AVP) video was shown to the faculty, featuring greetings and messages from the students, followed by the students who presented gifts to their beloved tutors.
Lastly, another raffle event was held with prizes for the faculty, which filled the room with joy and laughter.
The said event was supposed to be celebrated on October 3rd but was rescheduled due to Typhoon Paolo.| via Jasmine Zeah Llagas/The FOREFRONT

# GC Welcomes 5 new Registered Midwifes, Perfect passing in November MLE
[Image: GC Welcomes 5 new Registered Midwifes, Perfect passing in November MLE]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, November 19, 2025**
Five alumni from the College of Allied Health and Services (CAHS) of Gordon College (GC) passed the Midwives Licensure Exam (MLE) according to the Professional Regulation Commission Board.
GC achieved a perfect passing rate of 100% with 5 passers out of 5 examinees, once again surpassing the rate of last year’s results of 93.48% and the national rate of 57.60%. They constitute the 2,152 newly registered midwives.
All are first-time takers of this year’s exam.
Additionally, Tagaloan Community College is the top-performing school in the licensure exam with a perfect passing rate from 85 examinees of the school.
Meanwhile, Princesa Dumale Talamayan, this year’s board topnotcher, from Palawan State University achieved a 94.00%.
The licensure exam was held on November 8-9 and 16. | via Roi Chester B. Manio/The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College Named Top 4 Performing School in Feb 2026 NLE with 91.67% Passing Rate
[Image: Gordon College Named Top 4 Performing School in Feb 2026 NLE with 91.67% Passing Rate]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, March 7, 2026**
Huge congratulations to the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) for its outstanding performance in the recently conducted Nursing Licensure Examination (NLE) held on February 26–27, 2026. Gordon College has officially secured its spot as the TOP 4 PERFORMING SCHOOL with a remarkable institutional passing rate of 91.67%. This achievement is particularly exceptional as it more than doubles the National Passing Rate of 44.24%, proving once again that our educational standards are truly world-class. Alongside this institutional success, we celebrate the individual brilliance of Ms. Alexis Molon Penuliar, RN, for clinching the TOP 9 spot with a stellar rating of 89.80%.
To all our newly Registered Nurses, we are incredibly proud of your hard work and dedication!
This milestone is a testament to the unwavering support and visionary leadership of Hon. Rolen C. Paulino, Jr., City Mayor; Hon. Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino, Chairperson of the Committee on Education; Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, College President; and our dedicated academic leaders: Dr. Darwin P. Paguio (VPAA), Dr. Victor C. Quimen Jr. (CAHS Dean), Ms. Sheila Marie P. Oconer (CAHS Assistant Dean), and Ms. April Joyce Liban-Dela Cruz (BSN Program Chair). We also extend our deepest gratitude to our BSN faculty, city and college officials, personnel, and all stakeholders who continue to champion the [#TatakGC](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/tatakgc?__eep__=6&__cft__[0]=AZZLAviAlD9K56cxmCQ4Hp0lNtJRPIw-Oc4ZcWkzR32HmvBwC2s9FWXgXILNoZZMOIsEPRYeEHK0FIjcObA3-lm_3p0KB5QxPWZsB7vhwM_MfKHBR9ozJw7eZ8WNbOaQE2WB8Wv7dt05otJKLdGuH3l4&__tn__=*NK-R) brand of excellence.

[Image: Shift to Online Classes (July 19, 2025)]

July 18, 2025
SHIFT TO ONLINE CLASSES ADVISORY

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/galaxies-of-unity-ccs-students-explore-new-worlds-together

# Galaxies of Unity: CCS Students Explore New Worlds Together
[Image: Galaxies of Unity: CCS Students Explore New Worlds Together]
[CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [ForeFront](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/forefront/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, September 14, 2025**
ASINAN COVERED COURT, OLONGAPO CITY—The College of Computer Studies (CCS) of Gordon College launched its academic year with a universe of energy at the annual General Orientation and Acquaintance Party, “Galaxies of Unity: Exploring New Worlds Together,” held on September 13, 2025, at the Asinan Covered Court.
The event aims to transform into a constellation of colors, performances, advocacies, and camaraderie, welcoming both new and returning students into a community built on diversity, inclusion, and shared growth.
Furthermore, carrying the banner “Innovate, Include, Inspire: Pioneering Equality and Diversity in the AI-Driven World,” the orientation extended beyond the usual acquaintance party.
The event, spearheaded by Dean Ronnie Luy and Assistant Dean Armilyn Martinez with the support of the CCS faculty and coordinators, united students from the three CCS undergraduate programs—Bachelor of Science in Computer Science (BSCS), Bachelor of Science in Information Technology (BSIT), and Bachelor of Science in Entertainment and Multimedia Computing (BSEMC)—in celebrating inclusivity, teamwork, and talent.
“This event is not just for acquaintances, but also for orientation and presentation of our organizations and sportsfest participants. We want freshmen to feel welcome and informed, and for everyone to know the CCS community they belong to,” shared Ms. Yheng Miel Sanchez, CCS faculty.
At the heart of the program was the introduction of eight planetary teams, each representing a social cause: Lumora for Accessibility and Compassion (Persons with Disabilities), Veltraxis for Collaboration (Ethnicity and Race), Aurelia for Empathy (Senior Citizens), Cryonex for Innovation (AI and Robotics), Floraxis for Sustainability and Culture (Cultural Heritage), Kahelion for Resilience and Advocacy (Frontline Workers), Nyxara for Identity (LGBTQIA+), and Zenthara for Equity (Women Empowerment and Gender Equity).
Moreover, the day opened with an invocation, welcome remarks, and the general assembly before teams were formally revealed. Excitement built as each segment unfolded: cosplay presentations highlighted student creativity and confidence; production numbers filled the venue with music and dance; interactive games such as Constellation Connection, Bring Me, Inclusion Mosaic, One Word Explains All, Voices of the Galaxy, and The Matrix sparked laughter and teamwork; entrepreneurial booths offered handmade crochets, stickers, badges, and keychains; Echoes of Unity, the team chant presentations, raised school spirit; and raffle draws added surprise and fun throughout the day.
“Okay lang naman yung event, nakakapagod pero enjoyable. Pinakagusto ko yung cosplay, because makikita mo yung confidence and effort ng mga sumali,” said Jerry Andrew Famanila, CCS Student.
The cheers peaked with the introduction of players for CCS Sportsfest 2025, closing with the fiery chant, “Go CCS Phoenix!”. Student organizations, including the CCS Student Council, Society of Programming Enthusiasts in Computer Science (SPECS), Empowered League of Information Technology Education Students (ELITES), and Innovative Multimedia Artists Guild of Empowered Students (IMAGES), ensured the program’s smooth flow by managing venue setup, technical aspects, and coordination.
Between lively intermissions, the spotlight turned to academic achievements. The Dean’s Lister Awarding Ceremony honored the Top 10 achievers per program (BSCS, BSIT, BSEMC–Digital Animation, BSEMC–Game Development) and the Top 50 overall Dean’s Listers for A.Y. 2024–2025. Special recognitions, including Top Cosplayers, Best Production Number, and Winning Team Planet, added a celebratory flair, affirming that CCS nurtures both artistry and innovation.
In addition, John Derick De Leon, BSIT Student, shared that everything went according to the plan—which makes it very satisfying.
“Medyo mainit at may mga segment na biglaan pero doon lumalabas yung creativity ng students. Kitang-kita rin yung teamwork. Favorite ko yung part na bigla kaming pinag-drawing sa Inclusion Mosaic,” Mateo B. Bandilio, BSEMC student, shared.
Moreover, while the day brimmed with laughter, its deeper purpose was clear: to introduce freshmen to the CCS culture, strengthen bonds among students, and celebrate diversity as a source of strength. By merging advocacies with artistry and recognition with entertainment, the event became more than a kick-off celebration. It reaffirmed CCS’s mission to innovate, include, and inspire while promoting equality across differences.
As acknowledgments and final remarks drew the day to a close, ‘Galaxies of Unity’ stood as more than an acquaintance party. Guided by their advocacies, CCS students are set to carry the spirit of unity into the rest of the academic year, a reminder that while they may belong to different ‘planets’ of identity, talent, and cause, together they can form a universe of their own | Featured by Charyll Mae Cabungcal/The FOREFRONT

# GC APERTURA 2025
[Image: GC APERTURA 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, August 11, 2025**
GC APERTURA | Mayor Atty. Rolen C. Paulino Jr. warmly addressed more than 9,000 Gordon College students during the opening of the First Semester for Academic Year 2025–2026 held at the Tapinac Oval Grounds.
In his speech, the Mayor recognized Gordon College as one of Olongapo’s leading educational institutions, commending its role in shaping future professionals.
The event also featured the formal oath-taking of the Supreme Student Council officers for A.Y. 2024–2025, along with the officers of various student organizations and councils, officiated by Mayor Paulino Jr. himself.  
Courtesy to [**Olongapo City Information Center**](https://www.facebook.com/olongapopublicaffairs?__cft__[0]=AZUoEEuU7JRBp8m9YZ_lMtmfx9IJRxP901v3h3T0hfeZn1kxjLV0Z1r6Jfs3Bag4ifapazsLBegn1V1TQu1yB6pyZZx5ZTFUaZUlZqA2TOCdxP8_aqb4eGNYK49-vrS4sSl1XTuYTylL_EcYgARMMYYnCv7KUeZIwb2Y985F8OVk2jWSh389TouCTzffLa23nDTYIT4g6th1iQvxaJMV5SncNFYJ5OXzmrj-OwOukqkGvw&__tn__=-UC%2CP-y-R). # Mayor Atty. Rolen C. Paulino Jr. inspects the GC Annex Building
[Image: Mayor Atty. Rolen C. Paulino Jr. inspects the GC Annex Building]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, July 1, 2025**
Mayor Atty. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., together with the City Engineering Office, Office of the Building Official, Gordon College President Dr. Imelda DP Soriano, and the College’s Vice Presidents, conducted an inspection of the Gordon College Annex Building in Kalaklan.
The new facility features 17 classrooms, offices, and laboratories, and is scheduled to open this August in time for the new academic year, a significant addition to the city’s growing education infrastructure.

# NAME PROTECTED: Agaton and Cerilles Among the Philippines’ Most Outstanding
[Image: NAME PROTECTED: Agaton and Cerilles Among the Philippines’ Most Outstanding]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, August 27, 2025**
Two students from Aduana – Gordon College (GC) Chapter were recognized as the Most Outstanding BSCA Students of the Philippines for 2025 at an awards ceremony held by the Philippine Society of Customs Administration Students (PSCAS) Nationals on August 16 at Wind Residences, Tagaytay City.
Kris Rean Agaton was named the Top 1 Most Outstanding BSCA Student, while Renmmelle Kyle Cerilles secured the Top 3 position.
AGATON’S CUSTOMized JOURNEY
For Agaton, receiving the top honor was a moment of profound shock and validation.
She acknowledged experiencing personal doubt at the moment when she was nominated and considered her lack of extracurricular activities as a drawback compared to her fellow nominees across the country.
“I initially thought my chances of making it into the Top 10 were slim. That’s why, when I received the message that I ranked number one, I was overwhelmed with happiness and disbelief,” she added.
Furthermore, Agaton shared that her mother has always been the biggest source of motivation, as she’s been pushing her to move outside her comfort zone.
She revealed that, in her second year, she consistently declined opportunities for national competitions due to a “fear of failure.”
“Why not give it a try? It’s your last chance. I know you don’t regret turning down the chances before, but at least take this one as something you can look back on in the future,” her mother assured.
This encouragement helped her overcome her fear of failure.
CE-REALEST AND DEDICATION
On the other hand, Cerilles’s journey to the Top 3 position was a story of significant personal transformation. He admitted that he was far from what many would call ‘outstanding’ and did not always take academics as seriously as he should have.
“Honestly, if you look back at past years, I was far from what many would call ‘outstanding.’ I used to be the type of student who would rather stand outside the classroom,” Cerilles shared.
It was deliberate of him to give his best in whatever he did, and this eventually resulted in a leadership position at PSCAS-GC that took him to positions he never expected.
“This award is not just a recognition of what I achieved, but a reflection of how far I’ve come. From someone unsure of their place to someone willing to go beyond their borders,” he reflected.
Similarly, Cerilles attributed his success to the lessons and sacrifices of his parents.
He sees them as his “greatest mentors,” with his mother’s resilience and his father’s resourcefulness shaping his character and approach to challenges.
“They reminded me that success is never about individual effort alone, it is built upon the values, discipline, and character that we learn from those who raised us,” he emphasized.
When the two were asked what they felt was the most valuable piece of advice to give to other aspiring students of the Bachelor of Science in Customs Administration, both Agaton and Cerilles returned with bits of wisdom based primarily on their own experiences.
The advice given by Agaton focused on being aware of oneself and being focused.
The cluster I valedictorian urged students to remember their “why”—the reason they started—and to make that their anchor.
“Your journey is your own. Stay focused, be patient with yourself, and trust that your efforts—no matter how big or small—are leading you somewhere meaningful,” she reminded.
Meanwhile, Cerilles’ message was a call to “move beyond borders.”
Using the metaphor of an “aduana” (customs), he encouraged students to be brave enough to step out of their comfort zones.
Whether it is joining a student organization or taking academics more seriously, it brings one closer to discovering one’s true potential, he advised the students that growth only happens when you dare to cross those borders. | via Princess Alliyah Danday/The FOREFRONT
Photo Credits to PSCAS – Gordon College’s Official Facebook Page

# CEAS Mentors Women’s Doubles Clear Past CHTM Sharks for the Win
[Image: CEAS Mentors Women’s Doubles Clear Past CHTM Sharks for the Win]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, September 17, 2025**
TRICKSHOT BADMINTON COURT—Flora Amor Francisco and Vherna Lei Castañada of the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) made a powerful smash to victory in the Women’s Double category against the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) with a score of 21-10.
The Trickshot Badminton Court echoed with loud smashes and cheers as Francisco and Castañada dominated the game, leaving the CHTM Sharks with no chance at victory. The first set ended decisively, 21-4.
Francisco expressed her thrill and happiness during the interview as they were the first to win gold for the team.
“We know na ‘yon ‘yung way para lumakas ‘yung loob ng ibang ka-team namin, para lumaban at manalo para maibalik sa CEAS Badminton ang overall. Also, I promised before I graduated na maibalik ang overall champ sa CEAS Badminton,” the victor shared.
Francisco also expressed the challenges they faced in synchronizing their movements during matches, emphasizing how it is one of the most important aspects of playing doubles—where victory depends on two players moving as one.
In the elimination round, the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Sharks battled fiercely with the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Wizards, with the Sharks taking the win.
In the semifinals, the College of Computer Studies (CCS) Phoenix faced the CEAS Mentors, while the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Tigers battled the CHTM Sharks, setting up a championship showdown between CHTM and CEAS.
For the final standings, CAHS Tigers secured third place, CCS Phoenix finished in fourth place, and CBA Wizards settled into fifth place. | via Jeliza Tadena/The FOREFRONT

# 𝐃𝐫. 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐮𝐢𝐨 𝐨𝐧 𝐆𝐂’𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐜 𝐋𝐚𝐛: 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐙𝐚𝐦𝐛𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐬, 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐑𝐞𝐠𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐈𝐈𝐈
[Image: 𝐃𝐫. 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐮𝐢𝐨 𝐨𝐧 𝐆𝐂’𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐜 𝐋𝐚𝐛: 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐙𝐚𝐦𝐛𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐬, 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐑𝐞𝐠𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐈𝐈𝐈]
[Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, May 22, 2025**
Gordon College (GC), through the College of Computer Studies (CCS), opened its Mac Laboratory on the 5th floor of the building, room 507, last April 28.
This new facility aims to equip students with the necessary competencies to keep up with the quick growth of technological advancements.
According to Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, the College President, the Mac Laboratory aims to innovate the school through integrating smart classrooms over the years and to, ideally, attract international students, preparing the students for the evolution of Gordon College into a university.
Meanwhile, in a previous report by The Forefront, students from CCS initiated the GC CCS Tech Fest as a fundraising event last February for the improvements of the Mac Lab room and some of its equipment.
When interviewed again, Kurt Jeehan Terado, one of the event’s heads, expressed his joy because their collective effort as students manifested in their school.
“Knowing that we were able to contribute something that future students will benefit from, I feel like everyone from our batch felt fulfilled,” said Terado.
To add to their efforts, the Mac Lab units came from the Local Government Unit (LGU) as the funds raised through this event are for the sole-purpose of room improvements.
Moreover, Dr. Darwin Paguio, Vice President of Academic Affairs, declared his pride for GC’s achievement.
“The first local college na magkaka-Mac laboratory. [The] first in zambales, [the] first in region 3,” Dr. Paguio stated.
With GC’s climb towards innovation, the institution also eyes international deployment of its students in Macau.

# Artistry and Unity Take Center Stage at PASE Community Concert 2026
[Image: Artistry and Unity Take Center Stage at PASE Community Concert 2026]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, February 25, 2026**
The PASE Community Concert 2026, held on the 2nd day of the 27th Founding Anniversary celebration and in observance of National Arts Month, became a memorable showcase of talent and creativity. The program featured performances from Pase Ballo, Pase Voci, Pase Teatro, and Pase Cuerdas.
The concert opened with dance performances from PASE Ballo, followed by a singing presentation from PASE Voci, and an acting performance from PASE Teatro, with each group having its own spotlight to showcase their artistry and skills. It marked the first time that all PASE groups performed together on one stage while highlighting their unique talents individually.
The presentation celebrated unity, creativity, and the passion for the arts, making the community concert a meaningful part of the anniversary and National Arts Month celebration.
[#GC27thFoundingAnniversary](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/gc27thfoundinganniversary?__eep__=6&__cft__[0]=AZbUr141jeNw4wjVTqnCGysJpGdco8gMZQ2G5JeLy5a1KYqt6o3tiTWd9dixozSbNGgkLR6BT73PhtWYSssjRAD0RuxZLgoShKWRLIY2MpZarMNMQ9JHNZMa7KibFgTMcEA0ndNsbCJ7vBqtKpEisILqlNwFYEQc2-2PK8Jf_bLyw2Mzy2V8asasY_CbDlQbRkI&__tn__=*NK-R)

**Friday, April 25, 2025**
“𝘛𝘰 𝘣𝘦 𝘢 𝘤𝘩𝘢𝘮𝘱𝘪𝘰𝘯, 𝙘𝙤𝙢𝙥𝙚𝙩𝙚; 𝘵𝘰 𝘣𝘦 𝘢 𝘨𝘳𝘦𝘢𝘵 𝘤𝘩𝘢𝘮𝘱𝘪𝘰𝘯, 𝙘𝙤𝙢𝙥𝙚𝙩𝙚 𝙬𝙞𝙩𝙝 𝙩𝙝𝙚 𝙗𝙚𝙨𝙩; 𝒃𝒖𝒕 𝒕𝒐 𝒃𝒆 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝒈𝒓𝒆𝒂𝒕𝒆𝒔𝒕 𝒄𝒉𝒂𝒎𝒑𝒊𝒐𝒏, 𝙘𝙤𝙢𝙥𝙚𝙩𝙚 𝙬𝙞𝙩𝙝 𝙮𝙤𝙪𝙧𝙨𝙚𝙡𝙛.”
– 𝐌𝐚𝐭𝐬𝐡𝐨𝐧𝐚 𝐃𝐡𝐥𝐢𝐰𝐚𝐲𝐨
𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝘀 to our College of Education, Arts, and Sciences student representative—𝗠𝗿. 𝗝𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗵𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝗔. 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗮𝘀, 𝗟𝘂𝗰𝗸𝘆 𝗨𝗲𝗹 𝗔. 𝗠𝗮𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗮, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗠𝘀. 𝗟𝗼𝘃𝗲 𝗟𝗼𝗿𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗻𝗲 𝗩𝗲𝗻𝘂𝘀 𝗣. 𝗔𝗰𝗲𝗱𝗼, as well as their coaches 𝗠𝘀. 𝗝𝗲𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝗗𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗮𝗱𝗼 and 𝗠𝗿. 𝗖𝗵𝗿𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗟𝗼𝗶𝗱 𝗩𝗮𝗹𝗲𝗻𝘇𝘂𝗲𝗹𝗮, for being 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝗻 𝗞𝗢𝗟𝗘𝗛𝗜𝗬𝗔𝗪 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿-𝘀𝗰𝗵𝗼𝗼𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝗯𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟱 𝗮𝘁 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗮𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗴𝗲.
They have also won and received the following awards:
2𝒏𝒅 𝑩𝒆𝒔𝒕 𝑫𝒆𝒃𝒂𝒕𝒆𝒓 — 𝐋𝐨𝐯𝐞 𝐋𝐨𝐫𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐧𝐞 𝐕𝐞𝐧𝐮𝐬 𝐏. 𝐀𝐜𝐞𝐝𝐨

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-records-3127-freshmen-for-new-academic-year

# GC Records 3,127 Freshmen for New Academic Year
[Image: GC Records 3,127 Freshmen for New Academic Year]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, October 18, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE MAIN CAMPUS —Gordon College (GC) experiences an increase in its total student population for Academic Year 2025–2026, highlighted by a significant surge in freshmen enrollment across all college departments.
According to the official report from the Office of the College Registrar, the total student population rose from 8,625 this Academic Year (A.Y.) 2025-2026, in contrast to the 7,505 recorded Gcians last 2024–2025.
Furthermore, the number of Level 1 students also recorded a drastic jump from 2,731 to 3,127 freshmen.
The College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) continues to lead as the largest department, with 2,431 total students and 767 freshmen for this academic year.
The College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) recorded a population of 1,642 students and 676 freshmen.
The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) also experienced a notable increase in freshmen enrollees, from 468 last year to 663 this year.
While the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) saw an increase from 422 to 500 freshmen.
The College of Computer Studies (CCS) also recorded additional enrollees, welcoming 521 freshmen this year compared to the previous 442 record.
In line with its continuous growth, the CBA has officially moved into its new home at the GC Annex Building in Kalaklan, marking a new chapter for the college. | via Frances Marie Farne/The FOREFRONT

# Institutional Brigada Eskwela 2023
[Image: Institutional Brigada Eskwela 2023]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Thursday, August 17, 2023**
Brigada Eskwela served as a prime exemplar of how collaborative efforts across different sectors can enhance education in the Philippines. This program effectively united a diverse array of stakeholders to advance DepEd’s mission of ensuring equitable access to quality education for all Filipino learners.
In partnership with GC Student Organizations, the GC Community Extension Services Unit (GC-CESU) and the Office of Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) demonstrated unwavering dedication to providing vital community services. A significant objective of the institution was to create a conducive learning environment for students. Consequently, the GC community wholeheartedly participated in Brigada Eskwela 2023 (Brigada Clean-Up Drive), which yielded remarkable outcomes. Numerous Gordon College student organizations and academic staff from various programs actively contributed to this noble undertaking.

# Gordon College’s Newest Royalty Emerges
[Image: Gordon College’s Newest Royalty Emerges]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, February 24, 2026**
JUST IN | The spotlight bedazzles the center stage as Gordon College’s newest royalty emerges on stage. Mr. Marc Anthony Acula from College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) and Ms. Charmyn KC Sierra from College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) claim the crown as Mr. and Ms. Gordon College 2026.
These newly crowned icons are now set to carry the GCian pride to the regional and national stage, representing the institution in the Association of Local Colleges and Universities (ALCU) and the Local Colleges and Universities Athletic Association (LCUAA). | via Kana Maye Dueñas, The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College Joins the 2025 Times Higher Education Impact Rankings
[Image: Gordon College Joins the 2025 Times Higher Education Impact Rankings]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, June 18, 2025**
We are proud to announce that Gordon College has officially earned a place in the prestigious Times Higher Education (THE) Impact Rankings 2025! Despite being a newcomer to the global stage, GC was ranked among the top 1,500+ out of 2,526 higher education institutions (HEIs) from 130 countries, assessed for their contributions to achieving the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UN SDGs)—a recognition based on universities’ social impact through research, stewardship, outreach, and teaching.
Gordon College participated in four SDG categories and earned the following standings:
• SDG 3 – Good Health and Well-being: Ranked 1001–1500
• SDG 4 – Quality Education: Ranked 1501+
• SDG 10 – Reduced Inequalities: Ranked 1001+
• SDG 17 – Partnerships for the Goals: Ranked 1501+
More than just recognition, this milestone reflects our evolving vision—to become a globally recognized local institution making meaningful contributions to global progress through inclusive, equitable, and sustainable development in higher education.
[Image: 🔗]
View the full rankings here: [https://www.timeshighereducation.com/impactrankings](https://www.timeshighereducation.com/impactrankings?fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAYnJpZBExcTNkc2RsSjBLTDYxc2tHYwEejGKbxvzhMrtl29ZxRx90Hnq6JINWNlRYH9NAwdKPUl7niNP38O8MykS9cpg_aem_FulevGowcrEUlih87Sa6UQ)

# Responsibilities of the Data Subjects
Associated to the exercise of rights are responsibilities to respect the privacy of others and comply with Gordon College’s regulations in its administration of data privacy.
Data subjects shall have the following responsibilities:
1. Respect the data privacy rights of others;
2. Report any suspected security incident or personal data breach to Gordon College Data Protection Officer by sending an email or accomplishing OCP-DPU Form No. 4;
3. Provide Gordon College with true and accurate personal data and other information. Before submitting personal data of different people to Gordon College, obtain the consent of such people.
4. Not disclose to any unauthorized party any non-public confidential, sensitive or personal information obtained or learned in confidence from Gordon College; and
5. Abide by the policies, guidelines and rules of Gordon College on data privacy, information security, records management, research and ethical conduct. From time to time, check for and comply with updates on these policies, guidelines and rules. Students may also refer to their Student Handbook.
- ## NPC Registration
- [NPC Registration](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gordon-college-registrations-to-npc/)

# Website Policy
### Website Committee
Gordon College (GC) Website Committee was formally established by Interoffice Memorandum No. 38 Re: Website Committee. The Interoffice Memorandum states the following; composition, duties, and responsibilities of Website Committee, Guidelines in Website Content Development and Management, and Procedure for Posting/Uploading Web Content.
### Composition
GC Website Committee will be composed of a Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, Website Administrator, and Members.  
For the members, it will be the Head of the Information and Communication Services Unit (ICSU), one (1) faculty member from each department designated by the College Dean, and one (1) staff from each office/unit assigned by the Unit Head.
### Duties and Responsibilities of Website Committee
Based on Interoffice Memo No. 38, the following are the duties and responsibilities of the committee:
1. Develop and recommend web related policies and standards
2. Oversee the design and underlying technology
3. Ensure the website stays current with new technology/trends
4. Implement regular data collection methods
5. Use data collected to guide the framework and infrastructure of the college website
6. Create guiding principles to steer the framework and infrastructure decisions of the college website
7. Work with the Management Information System Unit (MISU) to set timelines for web engine upgrade and new website deployments.
8. Regularly assess the design of the website and make recommendations for improvement
9. Serve as the liaison between the College President and the different department regards content
10. Identify and advocate for web needs and services for the college community.
### Guidelines in Website Content Development and Management
1. Interoffice Memo No. 38, clearly states the guidelines to be observed by the committee in terms of the contents for the website.
2. All news, updates and all other content to be posted on the official website should have clearance from concerned departments/ units and approval from the College President.
3. The Website Committee is tasked to manage the postings on the official website in coordination with the Information and Communication Services Unit (ICSU). The committee shall formulate posting guidelines and ensure proper dissemination.
4. Queries, concerns or issues aired through the official website must be communicated to concerned departments through ICSU who shall also responsible for posting official responses to such queries, concerns or issues whether through online or offline as the situation warrants.
### Procedure for Posting/Uploading Web Content
The following are the guidelines stated in Interoffice Memo No. 38 in terms of posting/uploading content to the college website:
1. Requesting department should submit a copy of the post to ICSU for approval of the College President.
2. After approval, ICSU shall transmit the content to the Website Committee Chairperson and Web Administrator.
### Website Content Submission
The GC Website Committee is pleased to offer the opportunity to share knowledge and expertise by submitting content to the college website with the following guidelines:
* **Guiding Principles**  
  Gordon College is one of the institutions that promote excellent education in Region III and the country. As such, the committee would like to publish and post content, materials, and ideas that support the vision, mission, and goals of the college. The committee also encourages each individual from different colleges and departments to share their experience and insights as one of the people of Gordon College.
* **Style**  
  The Website committee encourages each and everyone to use the current best practices for web writing. These includes:
  + Begin with the conclusion by focusing on the main message and be clear on how you want the reader to feel and respond.
  + Add specific details, facts, figures, quotes, etc.
  + Submit at least one (1) graphs, graphics, or photos that are copyright free with a size of;
    - 1280 pixels by 720 pixels for Hero Banners (Congratulatory Posters)
    - 1200 pixels by 630 pixels for News, Announcement and Notice
    - 250 pixels by 250 pixels for Website Banner
    - 250 pixels by 100 pixels for Logo (Rectangle)
    - 100 pixels by 100 pixels for Logo (Square)
  + Graphs, graphics or photos should be in .jpeg, .png or .svg format
  + Use subheadings that reflect the content and captures the interests of readers
  + Use listed points with ten (10) words or less per item
  + Search the web for other pro tips on best practices
* **Publishing**  
  The committee does not guarantee that it will post your submissions automatically. All published contents shall be well-guided and must receive the approval of concerned departments, as stated on Interoffice Memorandum No. 38.It does guarantee, however, that the article will be read and explore whether or not it is a good fit for the college and the readership. In reviewing submissions, the committee is looking for content that is necessary and relevant to the needs of the stakeholders, especially with the faculty and students. Articles reflecting the inspiring, powerful, responsible, productive, and connected nature of the academe are the ones that we are looking for.
* **Copyright**  
  The committee will only post copyright-free materials submitted by the author and will not pay for articles posted on the website. The college reserves the right to use the submitted article or any part of it depending on the committee’s discretion.
* **Response Time**  
  The goal of the committee is to respond to the requestor as quickly as possible. Depending on the situation, the committee will do its best to respond to each submission in 1 to 3 days.
* **Requestor**  
  The following groups and/or individuals are allowed to submit content for posting:
  + Department Heads
  + Vice Presidents
  + Faculty Members and
  + Administrative Staff
  + Recognized Student Organizations
  + Alumni
### Specific Guidelines
To achieve uniformity and establish the visual identity of the college kindly follow the following in submitting your content;
* **Title and Author Information**  
  All submission must identify (to be written in the body of your message);
  + Topic
  + Title
  + Author’s Full name
  + Author’s Phone Number\*
  + Author’s Email Address\*
  + Department (if applicable)  
    *\* – needed for communication purposes*
* **Summary**  
  Provide a 20-30 words summary of the article that can be used for links.
* **Length of the Content**  
  Submit articles that are between 500-750 words. Please submit final drafts that have proofread, and grammar and spelling checked.  
  Do not include the graphics or photos within the document. It should be saved separately in the same folder where the file resides.
* **Formatting**  
  Send your document at **biron.neilmarc@gordoncollege.edu.ph** with the following format details:
  + Subject : Request for Posting (Website)
  + Body : Author’s Information
  + Topic : Event/ Celebration
  + Title : Sportsfest
  + Author’s Full name : Juan Dela Cruz
  + Author’s Phone Number : 607.7175
  + Author’s Email Address : juandelacruz@gmail.com
  + Department : College of Allied Health Studies
  + Summary of your article.
  + Attachment:
    - document file of your article
    - picture1.jpg\* / picture1.png\* / picture1.svg\*  
      *\* – you can select one particular image type*
* **Links**  
  Provide a list of referenced links in the text or at the end of the submitted article. The committee will review these links to determine if they are appropriate for the website and will determine if they will be included or not (especially for copyright acknowledgment).
* **Categorization of Submission**  
  Articles that will be posted in the website can be categorized into;
  + News
  + Announcement or Guidelines
  + Notice or Advisory
* **Tags**  
  This will be use to sub-categorized your submission;
  + CAHS *(College of Allied Health Studies)*
  + CCS *(College of Computer Studies)*
  + CBA *(College of Business and Accountancy)*
  + CEAS *(College of Education, Arts, and Sciences)*
  + CHTM *(College of Hospitality and Tourism Management)*
  + IGS (*Institute of Graduate Studies)*
  + OSWS*(Office of Student Welfare and Services)*
  + Campus
  + Alumni
  + Administration
  + Academics
  + Forefront
### Content Administration
All web content submitted must be approved prior to posting. The following individuals retain the right to edit or request changes:
* College President
* Website Administrator
* Head of ICSU
* Vice Presidents
* Requestor/Author of the Content
### Website Content Update/ Revision
Thou postings are checked for spelling, grammar and factual accuracy, there will be some instances that can result unwanted errors.
The GC Website Committee will allow update or revision of articles posted on the website for the following reasons;
* Missing or Duplicate Titles
* Misspelled names
* Overlooked misspelled words or grammatical errors
* Missing images or links
* Missing details or information
### Website Content Removal
The GC Website Committee reserves the right to remove any posted content that contains copyrighted materials which include, but not limited to: text, images, graphics, sound recordings, video recordings or software programs.

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/ceas-mentors-ibinida-ang-ginto-sa-standard-category

# CEAS Mentors, ibinida ang ginto sa Standard Category
[Image: CEAS Mentors, ibinida ang ginto sa Standard Category]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, September 15, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE, OLONGAPO CITY – Umangat ang kahusayan ng College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors sa taunang Dance Sports Competition Standard Category, matapos matagumpay na hinataw ang dalawang katunggali sa entablado sa pamamagitan ng tatlong klasikong sayaw.
Sinubukan ang kanilang husay at indak sa Waltz na nagpakita ng pino at walang-kupas na galaw, sa Tango na umigting sa matinding ekspresyon, at Quickstep na sumubok sa bilis, koordinasyon, at tibay ng bawat pares.
Sa kabila ng dikit na laban, naging kapansin-pansin ang stage presence, chemistry, kontrolado ngunit makapangyarihang galaw ni Jieann Cahanding at Ivan Russel Bahala mula sa CEAS Mentors na nagpakita ng malinis na footwork hanggang sa makabagbag na emosyon sa entablado. Sinundan ito ng CHTM (College of Hospitality and Tourism Management) Sharks sa ikalawang pwesto at CAHS (College of Allied and Health Studies) Tigers sa ikatlong pwesto.
“Aside from being student athletes, of course. Kami kasi ng partner ko first time partner, yung adjustment doon na tinuturuan ko siya kasi hindi rin siya talaga dance sport athlete, pero yung nakakamangha sa kaniya ay madali siya turuan, saad ni Jieann ukol sa hinarap nilang problema bago tumungtong sa entablado.
“Yung struggle namin ay ‘yong time management medyo mahirap kasi tuwing hapon lang kami nakakapag practice,” dagdag pa niya.
“Sobrang saya po na nakakaiyak. As a first-timer malaking challenge sa akin yun at malaking achievement,” ayon kay Bahala patungkol sa naramdaman matapos sungkitin ang kampeyonato.
Sa pagtatapos ng patimpalak, hindi lamang talento sa pagsasayaw ang naipakita ng mga kalahok. Bawat kolehiyo ay nagbigay ng kani-kaniyang tatak—mula sa makukulay na costume, malinis na footwork, hanggang sa mga koreograpiyang may hatid na emosyon at kwentong Gcians. | via Lianne Malazarte/The FOREFRONT
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/ccs-phoenix-clinches-gold-at-siklab-2025-e-games

# CCS Phoenix Clinches Gold at SIKLAB 2025 E-Games
[Image: CCS Phoenix Clinches Gold at SIKLAB 2025 E-Games]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, September 17, 2025**
FUNCTION HALL, GORDON COLLEGE MAIN BLDG.—The Gordon College Main Building Function Hall buzzed with excitement as SIKLAB 2025 E-Games came to a spectacular close, featuring intense battles for gold, silver, and bronze.
In the Battle for Bronze, the College of Education, Arts, and Science (CEAS) Mentors defeated the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Tigers, 22-8, securing third place and bringing pride to the CEAS community.
Meanwhile, the championship series between the CCS Phoenix and the CHTM Sharks was a nail-biter in a best-of-three format. CCS edged CHTM in Game 1, 21-20, followed by a 17-win in Game 2. In the final showdown, the Phoenix dominated, 12-1, clinching the gold medal with an overall 2-1 victory.
The CHTM Sharks earned the silver medal, demonstrating remarkable perseverance and strategy throughout the finals.
SIKLAB 2025 E-Games concluded as a remarkable showcase of determination, strategy, and camaraderie, crowning the CCS Phoenix as champions while bringing the excitement of competitive E-sports to Gordon College—and leaving fans eagerly anticipating next year’s edition. | via Frances Marie Farne/The FOREFRONT

# SCHEDULE OF ENROLMENT (Undergraduate Level)
[Image: SCHEDULE OF ENROLMENT (Undergraduate Level)]
**Wednesday, November 26, 2025**
**Second Semester, A.Y. 2025-2026**
**Online Clearance Processing**
Dec. 1– 19, 2025
Online Enlistment for Enrolment
(for Continuing and Readmitted Students)
Dec. 8, 2025 – Jan. 9, 2026
Application for Readmission and Change in Program
(Walk-in Applications only)
Jan. 5-14, 2026
Start of Classes
January 19, 2026
Notes:
· CAHS students shall follow the manual clearance process. Clearance forms are available at the Office of the Registrar.
· Students cannot proceed with the enlistment process until cleared of all accountabilities.
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
**ONLINE ENLISTMENT FOR ENROLMENT PROCEDURE**
1. Log-on to the GCES portal: ([https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gces/student](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fgordoncollegeccs.edu.ph%2Fgces%2Fstudent%3Ffbclid%3DIwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAYnJpZBExYmtONXprRWlaY05XUDNlanNydGMGYXBwX2lkEDIyMjAzOTE3ODgyMDA4OTIAAR79DMwGOjURxgNtRNM48cNS14RZiQ03STk68zaq7Y7S38vvJxITV_-rwzIGXg_aem_7mI144fGqXu0N5q8QyMZdg&h=AT1IVkhOy2caj4FsOiZC6KtLcGS7CF_3aUEBr6YBiyDYrjeSV9jJmVnM3bvjtkLS9ubcybZBpCpmOIqF60ee1ATknqEkHUk7yk2IUuEBMgz49kgkahymCpB1C3ojTSon_CF7Xs8nSOkcRQ9O&__tn__=-UK-R&c[0]=AT0Td0sy4zKeSU-3h2cCfuXxcqiavVsIeutqEmIOq2vtU2iE4XHgcumSKzPZumwRz3xxrqzzZP5WhGb8FaQXp6B1x9EC7ds_pqrDxh3f4dMiTJO2HLZtbFeZeD65jIeH5Buamysrdisv8_WNYv-ZDzY71au_BX0n04ocFacFP6YxOgF_HZTOMNEUG9ApGJ7CxnI38dyCP3iXNW_PCf0UYA9JPA))
A “forgot password request” option is available in case of trouble logging in.
2. Check the Dashboard for the online clearance status.
You will not be able to proceed with the enlistment process until you are cleared of all accountabilities.
3. View your academic records in the following menu:
a. Term Grade
b. Summary of Grades
c. Academic Evaluation Sheet
4. Click Profile/Enlistment.
To request for change of information, visit the Office of the Registrar.
5. Update academic and enlistment information.
6. Select preferred courses to enroll.
7. Read the Gordon College General Data Privacy Notice and click the CHECKBOX.
8. Click “ENLIST” button.
9. Completing all the steps above will register you for enrolment.
Your schedule of classes will be posted in your GCES account on or before the start of classes. You may ask the assistance of your program coordinator for class schedule matters.

# Bureau of Customs Port of Subic Recognized GC as Their Valued Academic Partner
[Image: Bureau of Customs Port of Subic Recognized GC as Their Valued Academic Partner]
[CBA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/cba/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, April 10, 2025**
We are truly honored to be recognized by the Bureau of Customs Port of Subic as one of your valued academic partners. Thank you for the continued trust and for providing our students with enriching real-world learning experiences.
This collaboration reflects the power of a strong public-academe partnership, shaping future professionals and reinforcing our shared commitment to nation-building through quality education.
Here’s to more meaningful opportunities ahead!
[Image: 📸]
In the photos: Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano (College President), Mr. John Angelo Diwa (OIC-Dean, College of Business and Accountancy), BSCA alumni, and students.

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/stude-orgs-answer-gcs-soae-call

# Stude orgs answer GC’s SOAE call
[Image: Stude orgs answer GC’s SOAE call]
[Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, February 26, 2025**
Despite the short attendance of student organizations and delegates in the GC 4th Student Organization Awards of Excellence (SOAE), the awarding ceremony proceeded in citing its outstanding student organizations and student-leaders in service for A.Y. 2024-2025 at GC Function Hall, yesterday.
The 4th SOAE awarded, in no particular order, its recipients of:
Top 3 Outstanding Student Organization | Interactive Organization of Natural Sciences (IONS), League of Tourism Students of the Philippines (LTSP) – Gordon College Chapter, and Samahang Filipino (SamFil)
Top 3 Outstanding Student Organization Adviser | Jehan B. Doblado, Kristoffer Paul C. Obispo, and Mychale Williams
Top 3 Outstanding Community Extension Program | CHTM Student Council, Interactive Organization of Natural Sciences (IONS), and Samahan ng mga Mag-aaral sa Araling Panlipunan
Meanwhile, Nicolas Milton Ching earned the Servant Student Leader of the Year while Klaudtrev Hanrick Payumo merited Rookie Student Servant of the Year. Jannae Mae De Jesus received the Student Organization Presidential Award and Sophia Joleen Evangelista with Outstanding Student Extensionist Award.
And in no particular order, this year’s Top 10 Outstanding Student Leaders were:
Nicolas Milton Ching | SPECS
Jhoemari Corpus | IONS
Janna Mae De Jesus | SMAP
La Arnie Morales | CHTM SC
Dhana Eunice Papa | JFINEX
Kharl Christopher Nofies | SAMFIL
Lorenzo Don Cayabyab | LTSP
Ralf Vincent Paule | PSCAS
John Kyle Perez | SAMFIL
Jay Angelo Manalang | LTSP
Aeros Raphael Morales | LELLS
Lynard Jay Ebalo | IONS
Mark Louis Macachor | CHTM SC
Sophia Joleen Evangelista | SMAP
Moreover, LTSP-GC Adviser Mychale Williams won the Student Organization Adviser of the Year.
The CHTM Student Council came out once more as the Student Council of the Year among others while IONS, after bagging two other awards earlier, also flexed on its Spirit of Service Award plaque while LTSP nagged the Student Organization of the Year title.
This ceremony is still in line with the celebration of the 26th Founding Anniversary of the institution.

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/announcement/official-statement-from-gordon-college

# OFFICIAL STATEMENT FROM GORDON COLLEGE
[Image: OFFICIAL STATEMENT FROM GORDON COLLEGE]
**Sunday, January 4, 2026**
Gordon College is aware of the report aired by Bilyonaryo News in January 2026 concerning an alleged ₱19 million overpayment related to the implementation of the Free Higher Education (FHE) program specifically the mismatched definition of laboratory rooms.
In connection with the Commission on Audit’s (COA) rejoinder dated March 14, 2025, regarding Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) No. CHED-CO-2025-003 concerning the Free Higher Education (FHE) reimbursements to Gordon College for Academic Years 2019-2020 to 2023-2024, the institution submitted its clarifications and justification in coordination with the UniFAST Secretariat and Commission on Audit last May 16, 2025.
The institution’s initial inclusion of the Audio-Visual Room (AVR) fees under laboratory fees was made with good intentions and full transparency. This long-standing policy was openly reflected in the institution’s submitted and approved Tuition and Other School Fees (TOSF), which listed the AVR for laboratory fees. However as early as last year, once informed that “laboratory fees” is strictly interpreted to relate to specific courses that use specialized equipment, which the previous classification may not have fully met, the College adjusted accordingly and removed the said fees from the list.
The College wishes to clarify that funds received under the Free Higher Education program are meticulously allocated and budgeted which undergoes strict audit by appropriate national government agencies, including the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) and UniFAST and the Commission on Audit. All collections approved by the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) – UniFAST were allocated transparently for the operations and development of the college.
Gordon College is fully cooperating with concerned government agencies and complying with the recommendations of the Commission on Audit. The institution has always upheld transparency, accountability, and the proper stewardship of public funds. We respectfully call on the public to allow due process to take its course and to rely on verified information from official sources. Gordon College remains committed to addressing this matter responsibly and will provide updates as soon as official findings are available.


### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/chtm-sharks-sealed-the-set-against-ceas-mentors-in-mens-singles

# CHTM Sharks sealed the set against CEAS Mentors in Men’s Singles
[Image: CHTM Sharks sealed the set against CEAS Mentors in Men’s Singles]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, September 17, 2025**
TRICKSHOT COVERED COURT—The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Sharks bagged the gold again during the men’s badminton singles championship match, 21-6, defeating the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors.
With his outstanding performance, Kryztofer Matias Basa of CHTM Sharks ended the finals with two straight sets, leaving no chance for his opponent to recover.
In an interview, Basa shared that he already expected the victory as he had once played with a stronger player before.
“‘Yu‘n pa yung ginamit ko na motivation para magchampion ulit yung CHTM,” he said when asked about being pressured to defend the previous win of CHTM last SIKLAB.
He also expressed his joy in playing with passion and humility. “Iniisip ko lang is maging humble, and…sportsmanship,” he added.
Moreover, the College of Computer Studies secured the bronze medal, while the College of Business and Accountancy and the College of Allied Health got the 4th and 5th place, respectively. | via Jeliza Tadena/The FOREFRONT

# GC receives COCPs of ALL of its undergraduate programs
[Image: GC receives COCPs of ALL of its undergraduate programs]
[Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, April 23, 2025**
In adherence to the directive of the Commission on Higher Education Regional Office III (CHEDRO III) mandating the conversion of Government Authority (GA) to Certificate of Program Compliance (COPC) for all academic programs, Gordon College is proud to announce that 100% of its undergraduate programs have successfully obtained COPC.
This accomplishment would not have been possible without the unwavering support of Dr. Lora L. Yusi, Regional Director of CHEDRO III. We extend our heartfelt gratitude for her invaluable guidance throughout this journey.
We also express our deepest appreciation to Hon. Rolen C. Paulino, City Mayor; Hon. Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino, Chairperson of the Committee on Education; Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, College President; Dr. Darwin P. Paguio, Vice President for Academic Affairs; the Board of Trustees Members, Vice Presidents, Directors, Heads, Deans, Assistant Deans, Program Coordinators, Faculty Members, Administrative Personnel, and all other stakeholders of the College whose collective efforts and commitment made this achievement possible.

# GC: Shortlisted for Technological or Digital Innovation of the Year (THE) Awards Asia 2025
[Image: GC: Shortlisted for Technological or Digital Innovation of the Year (THE) Awards Asia 2025]
[IPDEA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ipdea/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, April 24, 2025**
Gordon College is absolutely thrilled and incredibly proud to announce that we are one of the 8 finalists in the prestigious Times Higher Education (THE) Awards Asia 2025 under the category of Technological or Digital Innovation of the Year! “This award recognizes the innovative use of digital technology to improve any function at a university, be that at institutional, departmental or faculty level”.
This incredible recognition is for our pioneering Gordon College Learners’ Academic Management Portal (GCLAMP), a testament to our commitment to innovative and accessible education for our students.
We extend our deepest gratitude and congratulations to the entire Gordon College community, especially to the brilliant minds behind GCLAMP. Our College President, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, our Vice President for Institutional Planning, Development and External Affairs, Ms. Kristin Joy A. Mendoza, our Vice President for Administration and Finance and Project Leader of GCLAMP, Dr. Erlinda S. Casela-Abarintos, and our Coordinator of the International Affairs Unit, Ms. Precious S. De Castro, proudly represented Gordon College at the awards ceremony and the Asia Universities Summit in Macau, China from April 22-24, 2025.
A special kudos to everyone who has been part of the GCLAMP journey: the Commission on Higher Education Regional Office III (CHEDROIII) headed by Dr. Lora L. Yusi, our supportive City Mayor, Hon. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., the Chairperson of the Committee on Education, Hon. Cristabelle C. Paulino, the dedicated GCLAMP development team, all our hardworking college personnel, and our valued stakeholders. Your collective efforts and support have been instrumental in this achievement!
This milestone reflects Gordon College’s dedication to pushing boundaries and embracing technology to enhance the learning experience. We are honored to be recognized among the leading institutions in Asia.

# GC Color Run Kicks Off 27th Founding Anniversary
[Image: GC Color Run Kicks Off 27th Founding Anniversary]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, February 22, 2026**
The annual GC FUNdation RUN marked the start of Gordon College’s 27th Founding Anniversary, held on February 22, 2026, at the East Tapinac Oval Track.
The event gathered members of the Gordon College Faculty Association (GCFAS), administrative staff, students, alumni, and community participants under the pre-foundation day celebration motif, “Color Run.”
The program commenced with the assembly of participants, followed by an opening ceremony that featured welcome messages from Dr. Katherine Yap and Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano. A Zumba warm-up session, led by administrators, faculty members, and students from the Bachelor of Physical Education (BPED), energized attendees ahead of the race.
The run officially started at 6:30 AM as participants filled the track dressed in white shirts and athletic attire, ready to be splashed with vibrant colored powder and water.
More than a display of color and movement, this year’s FUNdation RUN is a colorful display of the institution’s commitment to promoting wellness, unity, and community relations while marking the upcoming foundation celebration with lively and memorable moments. | via Sam Andrea R. Delos Reyes/The FOREFRONT
Photos by Angela Samson, Samantha Suñga, and Rhianne Cuesta

# 𝐌𝐀𝐘𝐀𝐑𝐈 𝐑𝐢𝐬𝐞𝐬: 𝐆𝐂 𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐧𝐜𝐡𝐞𝐬 𝐟𝐢𝐫𝐬𝐭 𝐋𝐆𝐁𝐓𝐐𝐈𝐀+ 𝐒𝐭𝐮𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐎𝐫𝐠𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧
[Image: 𝐌𝐀𝐘𝐀𝐑𝐈 𝐑𝐢𝐬𝐞𝐬: 𝐆𝐂 𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐧𝐜𝐡𝐞𝐬 𝐟𝐢𝐫𝐬𝐭 𝐋𝐆𝐁𝐓𝐐𝐈𝐀+ 𝐒𝐭𝐮𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐎𝐫𝐠𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, June 18, 2025**
Gordon College established its first LGBTQIA+ Student Organization named “Mga Anak ng Yugtong Apo para sa Respeto at Inklusyon (MAYARI),” in line with the celebration of Pride Month this June.
MAYARI aims to champion the rights and well-being of LGBTQIA+ students within the college and beyond.
Furthermore, Mr. Marko Aratea, organization adviser of MAYARI, confirmed that the organization has developed rapidly and has profound objectives.
“We, at MAYARI, are currently in the process of becoming a formally recognized civic organization at Gordon College, but we will get there very soon!” Mr. Aratea stated.
Moreover, MAYARI was formed with both students and the Office of Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) in mind as a dedicated community for LGBTQIA+ students and their allies. Both Mr. Aratea and co-adviser Mr. Christian Loid Valenzuela started as the founding members who led the creation of the constitution, set clear objectives, and met institutional standards.
The central aim of MAYARI is to establish a safe and inclusive space for LGBTQIA+ students. Mr. Aratea outlined the organization’s primary objectives: “MAYARI aims to provide a safe and inclusive space for LGBTQIA+ students and allies in our humble institution. Our main goals are to promote visibility, inclusivity, respect, empowerment, and solidarity.”
MAYARI intends to accomplish these objectives through education by providing LGBTQIA+ seminars, webinars, gender sensitivity programs, and opportunities for creativity and leadership.
Furthermore, one of the important goals of MAYARI is to handle issues affecting LGBTQIA+ individuals, mainly discrimination, mental health challenges, and lack of community understanding.
“MAYARI is a civic organization for LGBTQIA+ and allies in GC, also serving as a platform to address catalysts related to gender-based concerns through formal dialogues programs and activities that will equip relevant stakeholders regarding said concerns and practical solutions,” MAYARI’s adviser affirmed.
Meanwhile, the organization will implement peer support initiatives, explore partnerships with the college’s Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) office, and launch extensive awareness campaigns on SOGIESC (Sexual Orientation, Gender Identity, Gender Expression, and Sex Characteristics).
Looking beyond symbolic gestures, MAYARI envisions a future where “total acceptance” is deeply ingrained in the institution’s policies and culture.
“Total acceptance means more than symbolic gestures—it involves policy and culture change,” Mr. Aratea emphasized and further discussed that the organization aims to integrate inclusivity into the college’s systemic operations rather than limiting it to mere statements.

# Gordon College Welcomes IYES Language School for Cultural Exchange and Nihongo Lesson
[Image: Gordon College Welcomes IYES Language School for Cultural Exchange and Nihongo Lesson]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, March 9, 2026**
Last March 6, 2026, Gordon College, through its International Affairs and Linkages Office, welcomed visitors from IYES Language School. Two Japanese students conducted an engaging educational and cultural exchange with 2nd Year BS Tourism Management students taking THP101 – Foreign Language 1 under Ms. Jacqueline Fabian, Instructor. The class visit featured a mini cultural presentation and an introductory Japanese (Nihongo) lesson, where the visiting students shared insights into Japanese culture, including common conversations and greetings, as well as interesting Japanese onomatopoeic expressions used in daily life. The highlight of the session was the interactive Q&A activity, where students enthusiastically exchanged questions and ideas, creating a lively and meaningful dialogue between cultures. This initiative aimed to foster cross-cultural understanding, provide opportunities for both visitors and students to share experiences and perspectives, and create a warm, interactive, and memorable learning experience for everyone involved.

# GC Ranks 3 in May 2025 PNLE; 2 Alumni Among Topnotchers
[Image: GC Ranks 3 in May 2025 PNLE; 2 Alumni Among Topnotchers]
[CAHS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/cahs/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, May 14, 2025**
Gordon College (GC) secures third spot among top performing schools in the May 2025 Philippine Nurses Licensure Examination (PNLE), earning a rating of 98.29%, as 115 out of 117 examinees successfully made the cut.
Moreover, two GC Alumni; Martin Jorge Bernal Alcantara and Loren Mirador Mistica, emerged as top 9, both acing a rate of 88.20%.
This milestone not only underscores the college’s commitment to academic excellence but also reinforces its standing as a premier institution for nursing education in the country.
