# BASURA, PASILIDAD, UNIPORME: Students Address Concerns In Town Hall Meeting
[Image: BASURA, PASILIDAD, UNIPORME: Students Address Concerns In Town Hall Meeting]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, November 11, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE FUNCTION HALL MAIN BLDG—In an effort to strengthen transparency and communication between the student body and the administration, various students and representatives from different organizations voice out their concerns and proposed solutions on pressing campus issues that affect their daily experiences on the Town Hall Meeting, spearheaded by the Supreme Student Council (SSC) of Gordon College (GC) on November 4, at Gordon College Function Hall Main Bldg.
Among the major issues discussed were concerns with the disappearance of trash bins, restroom malfunctions, and the absence of student lounges, which reflect the everyday struggles of GCians that often go unnoticed. While these may seem like small matters, they collectively define the comfort, cleanliness, and morale of the student body.
Meanwhile, other pressing matters, such as the condition of the main and annex buildings and inconsistent implementation of the dress code, point to deeper issues of transparency, inclusivity, and student welfare.
DISAPPEARANCE OF TRASH BINS: CLEANLINESS AND WASTE MANAGEMENT ISSUES RAISED
Campus cleanliness had been a pressing issue among students following the recent disappearance of trash bins across campus. Students reported that many areas, including restrooms, now lack accessible waste bins, leading to increased littering and cleanliness issues.
Participants suggest restoration of the trash bins throughout the campus to promote cleanliness and for students’ easier access, emphasizing that this could help maintain a clean environment around the campus.
BUILDING FUNCTIONALITY: CALLS FOR BETTER FACILITIES BOTH IN THE MAIN AND ANNEX BUILDINGS
Moreover, the Annex Building, which accommodates a growing number of classes, points to another major concern. Students described poor ventilation, unstable internet connectivity, and a lack of proper equipment—including the sound system in the Function Room—as recurring problems that lessen their productivity.
Furthermore, students also expressed frustrations over the lack of equipment in the Main Building, such as practicums and simulations.
The SSC noted the importance of these concerns and emphasized the need for facility upgrades to ensure a more conducive academic environment, emphasizing that as Gordon College aims to be a university, infrastructure and facility improvements must keep pace with academic demands.
DRESS CODE VIOLATIONS: STUDENTS CALL FOR CONSISTENCY AND CLARITY
One of the most discussed topics was the implementation of the college’s dress code policy. Several students expressed confusion over inconsistent enforcement across departments, citing instances of inconsistency and a lack of clear communication throughout the student body.
“Minsan po navi-violation kami kahit appropriate po ‘yung suot namin [na uniform],” one Bachelor of Science and Tourism Management (BSTM) Student expressed.
Participants also noted that some students are reprimanded for minor violations while others are not held to the same standard.
“Naka-org shirt po ako then ni-question po ako kung bakit [naka-org shirt]. Nag-explain po ako na may event, pero may nauna po sakin na naka-civilian [pero] ‘di po siya ni-question,” another student from College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) shared.
In the latter part of the meeting, Gcians requested clearer guidelines and a more uniform implementation across departments to help promote both discipline and inclusivity within the campus community, and avoid misunderstanding and unequal treatment.
STUDENT LOUNGE, RESTROOM, AND OTHER CONCERNS
The functionality and cleanliness of restrooms were also brought up as a long-standing concern. Some facilities were reported to have malfunctioning faucets and dysfunctional doors, toilets, and bidets. Students cite concerns for regular maintenance checks to ensure comfort and hygiene for all.
On the other hand, Participants also voiced out frustrations over the lack of student lounges and rest areas where they can relax, study, or collaborate during vacant periods. Many pointed out that the few available classrooms require permission to be used, forcing students to stay in hallways or stairwells in between classes.
One student from CHTM proposed designating student lounges on each floor to provide space for students as a resolution, citing that this would provide comfort for students.
The SSC assured attendees that all issues and recommendations raised will be compiled and formally addressed to the college administration for further deliberation with the BIBINGKA project, held on November 7, Friday.
The council also promised its commitment to continue facilitating such dialogues to ensure that students’ voices are not only heard but also acted upon. | via Love Lorraine Acedo/The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College receives Academic Excellence Award 2025
[Image: Gordon College receives Academic Excellence Award 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, December 9, 2025**
Gordon College receives the prestigious Academic Excellence Award from ALCUCOA for significantly demonstrating excellence in education through its outstanding performance in the recent Licensure Examinations conducted by the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC).
The award specifically recognizes the college’s exceptional results, which include:
1. November 2025 Midwifery Licensure Examination: Achieving a 100% passing rate for first-time takers, far exceeding the national average. This perfect score solidifies Gordon College’s reputation for producing highly competent health professionals.
2. May 2025 Philippine Nurses Licensure Examination (PNLE): Gordon College earns national recognition for producing top-ranking examinees.
Top 9 Topnotchers: Martin Jorge Bernal Alcantara and Loren Mirador Mistica
3. May 2025 Philippine Nurses Licensure Examination (PNLE): Also producing a top-ranking examinee:
Top 8 Topnotcher: Rose Sheilla Mae D. Domingo
This remarkable achievement in both the Midwifery and Nursing licensure exams underscores the effectiveness of Gordon College’s rigorous academic programs, the dedication of its faculty, and the quality of its students.
With this significant recognition, the college is reinvigorated and eager to pursue its commitment to providing quality higher education and continuing its mission to produce competent, industry-ready alumni who are well-equipped to excel in their respective fields.

**Tuesday, April 8, 2025**
Congratulations to Gordon College for being recognized by the Commission on Higher Education as one of the awardees of the “Excellence in Quality Assurance in Teacher Education (EQUATE) Awards,” held on April 2, 2025, at the Manila Hotel.
This prestigious award recognizes the institution’s unwavering commitment to upholding the highest standards in pre-service teacher education, compliance with CHED minimum standards, program accreditation, and outstanding performance in the Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT).

# RESCHEDULING OF ID PROCESSING (December 8, 2025)
[Image: RESCHEDULING OF ID PROCESSING (December 8, 2025)]
**Sunday, December 7, 2025**
[Image: 📢]
**NOTICE: RESCHEDULING OF ID PROCESSING**
Please be informed that the scheduled ID Processing on December 8, 2025 (Monday) has been rescheduled due to the non-working holiday.   
ID Processing will now be conducted on any day from December 9 to December 12, 2025 (Tuesday–Friday). Students may proceed on their preferred date within this period.   
We apologize for any inconvenience this may cause and appreciate your understanding.


### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/beauty-meets-sportsmanship-pislaan-and-pradas-crowned-as-mr-and-ms-sportsfest-2025

# Beauty Meets Sportsmanship: Pislaan and Pradas Crowned as Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest 2025
[Image: Beauty Meets Sportsmanship: Pislaan and Pradas Crowned as Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, September 15, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE, OLONGAPO CITY— Cheers filled the East Tapinac Oval Grounds as the perfect blend of charm and athletic spirit came to life with representatives from various colleges showcasing their poise and sportsmanship, on the recently concluded Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest 2025 on Monday, September 15.
Kick-starting off the event with a bang, representatives wowed the crowd with their charisma and creative display of athletic-themed fashion for the Best in Sportswear award, which went to Mr. Chester Pislaan and Ms. Bhavjeet Kaur L. Singh from the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors.
But the night didn’t end there—the battle for the ultimate title pushed the candidates to bring out their best poise, personality, and confidence. As the event drew to a close, the stage erupted in cheers and anticipation as the overall winners were finally announced.
After an intense showdown, Ms. Dhana Del C. Pradas from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Wizards and Mr. Chester Pislaan from the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) emerged as Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest—proving that talent, wit, and stage presence shine brightest when matched with determination and sportsmanship.
Furthermore, hot on their heels, the 1st Runner-Up titles went to Mr. Marc Anthony Acula (CBA) Wizards and Ms. Bhavjeet Kaur L. Singh (CEAS) Mentors, whose outstanding performances made them strong contenders throughout the competition.
Meanwhile, Mr. Krizztan Vic A. Dominico and Ms. Ron Eriele Joi P. Agustin from College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Tigers secured the 2nd Runner-Up spot, earning admiration for their dynamic partnership.
In 3rd place, Mr. Charles De Leon from College of Computer Studies (CCS) Phoenix and Ms. Alexandra Macky Alisbo from College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) claimed their rightful spot. At the same time, Mr. Jack Francis P. Meran (CHTM) Sharks and Ms. Janna Lyn Rolls, CCS Phoenix, completed the roster as the 4th place finishers. | via Love Lorainne Venus Acedo/The FOREFRONT

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory

# From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory
[Image: From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, March 1, 2026**
Accountancy students of Gordon College gathered at East Tapinac Covered Court on February 25 for Junior Philippine Institute of Accountants (JPIA)’s Ultimate Gamble Team Building.
The participants collaborated, competed, and conquered through strategy-driven games that tested teamwork and unity.
Demy Anne M. Roallos headed the Green Team (Berde Makausad), Triskelly Lianne Cyril Cabildo led the Red Team (Pulang Ina), Felicity Dyle Rivas guided the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca), Aran Benz C. Sardañas captained the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada), Shakira Nicole B. Guintu steered the Pink Team (cPINKa – Certified Pink Accountants), Jan Lester M. Bitangcol led the Purple Team (Ooh-Lila), and Hannah Jhem M. Malit directed the Blue Team (BIR – Bluereau of Impeccable Recorders).
As the event began, each team performed their energetic yells and presented their flags to start the event with a blast. In the first game entitled “Caterpillar Game,” Green Team (Berde Makausad) won first place, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) gained second place, and Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) took third place.
For the second game of “Pass the Balloon,” Red Team (Pulang Ina) achieved the first place, followed by the Pink Team (cPINKa), and lastly, the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca). As the third game commenced, entitled “The Floor is Lava,” Pink Team (cPINKa) was hailed as the champion, Green Team (Berde Makausad) as the first runner-up, and the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) as the second runner-up.
Moreover, in the station games, the teams played in seven stations, namely Pass the Ball, Cut it Up, Wrap-tastic, Balloon Cup Relay, Rollercoaster Ball, T-Shirt Relay, and Calcu Relay. Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) triumphed in first place, Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) succeeded in second place, while Pink Team (cPINKa) acquired third place.
After consolidating all performance entries like preparing a final financial statement, Pink Team (cPINKa) emerged as the overall champion with a total audited score of 185 points, followed by Green Team (Berde Makausad) with 180 points, Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) with 175 points, Blue Team (BIR) and Red Team (Pulang Ina) who tied with 170 points, and lastly, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) and Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) who both garnered 150 points. Furthermore, Blue Team (BIR) won for the best yell, while Red Team (Pulang Ina) claimed the best flag award.
According to Pink (cPINKa) team leader, Shakira Nicole B. Guintu, their victory came from proper planning before the activities and going all-in on collaboration. “No’ng nag-activity po kami, laging may nagsasalita and nakikinig. Pwedeng higher batch, pwedeng lower batch, basta lahat naman po kami nakikipag-cooperate,” said Guintu.
Guintu also added that the team building reflected real-world challenges in accountancy, helping them to be patient and to remain calm under pressure. “May activities na parang paulit-ulit na kami pero nire-remind namin na kalmahan lang, na i-go lang sa flow namin, sa pace namin,” the team leader stated.
The activity ultimately reinforced that future accountants must not only master numbers but also embody the 3 C’s—Collaborate, Compete, and Conquer—skills essential in navigating real-world professional engagements and organizational challenges. | via Justine Stephanie A. Canlas/The FOREFRONT

# Empowering Solo Parents: Gordon College Launches ‘Weave of Strength’ Initiative
[Image: Empowering Solo Parents: Gordon College Launches ‘Weave of Strength’ Initiative]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, April 8, 2026**
Empowering communities through sustainable livelihood and inclusive development, Weave of Strength: Turning Scraps into Sustainable Livelihood was conducted on April 7, 2026 at the Gordon College Function Hall. The initiative provided solo parents and economically disadvantaged individuals with practical skills in transforming recycled fabric scraps into sustainable sources of income.
The program featured an inspiring message from College President Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano and the support of Vice President for RDCES, Dr. Ediric D. Gadia, reinforcing the college’s commitment to community service and development.
With the support of the Rotary Club of Downtown Olongapo, and through the coordinated efforts of RDCES and the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management as the implementing unit, the initiative reflects Gordon College’s core values of Character, Excellence, and Service.
To ensure long-term sustainability, the initiative adopts a capacity-building approach by equipping administrative personnel to serve as future facilitators, enabling continuous implementation and expanded community reach.

# CHED validates the MAN program of Institute of Graduate Studies
[Image: CHED validates the MAN program of Institute of Graduate Studies]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, June 19, 2025**
The CHED Technical Panel for Nursing, Technical Evaluator for Nursing, and Regional Quality Assessment Team (RQAT) conducted onsite validation at Gordon College, Institute of Graduate Studies, last May 29-30, 2025.
The Gordon College welcomed evaluators from the Commission on Higher Education (CHED)—Regional Quality Assessment Team (RQAT) for an on-site validation aimed at securing a Certificate of Program Compliance (COPC) for the Master of Arts in Nursing (MAN) program.
Program Chair for MAN, Dr. Victor C. Quimen Jr., introduced the CHED-RQAT evaluators: Dr. Maria Martina Geraldine Dimalibot, Dr. Al D. Biag, Dr. Mary Ann Gienette D. Medina, and Ms. Niña Jessa Angeli Palaganas.
The evaluators conducted thorough assessments of program administration, faculty credentials, curriculum, student services, facilities, and resources. Their findings and recommendations, discussed in a post-evaluation conference, will guide Gordon College, Institute of Graduate Studies continuing efforts to enhance academic quality.
Preparations were led by Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, Dean of the Institute of Graduate Studies; Dr. Darwin P. Paguio, Vice President for Academic Affairs; Dr. Narcisa R. Figuerres, Assistant Dean of the Institute of Graduate Studies; and Dr. Victor C. Quimen Jr. Program Chair for MAN, Ms. Joan Joy A. De Vera; Executive Assistant of the Institute of Graduate Studies, Ms. Diana Grace M. Vallar; Executive Assistant of the Academic Affairs Office; and strong support from other college officials and faculty.
Gordon College, Institute of Graduate Studies, conveyed its sincere gratitude to the CHED-RQAT team for their invaluable insights and steadfast support, recognizing the visit as a major step in the institution’s ongoing journey toward academic excellence.

# TF marks history at 21st LHEPC; 3 scribes snatch indiv awards
[Image: TF marks history at 21st LHEPC; 3 scribes snatch indiv awards]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, March 22, 2026**
The Forefront (TF) delegates secured three individual awards, first time in the publication’s Luzon-wide stint, at the 21st Luzon-Wide Higher Education Press Conference (LHEPC) from March 17-19, at NGN Grand Hotel in Tuguegarao City.
Representing Central Luzon, the individual category participants bagged awards in: Traditional Literary Graphics Illustration (English), 3rd place, from Senior Layout Artist Angelique Jose; Head of Traditional Arts Mary Isobelle Arcilla secured 7th Place in Traditional Comic Strip Drawing (English); and John Christian Rocero, former social media manager, took home 9th place in Photojournalism (English).
At the same time, John Patrick Mateo, Editor-in-Chief, participated in Devcomm Writing (Filipino) while Lance Isaac Leon, Associate Editor, participated in Editorial Writing (English) and Column Writing (English).
Moreover, Region III was named the Best Performing Region for the Individual and Group categories.
Announced on the same day, LHEPC will be replaced next year by the 1st National Higher Education Press Conference (NHEPC), to be hosted by Region I at Vigan City.
Similar to LHEPC rules, the top 5 placers in each category from the respective regional presscons will advance to the NHEPC. | via Roi Chester B. Manio/ THE FOREFRONT
Graphics by Rich Mae Tianchon

# GC welcomes ALCUCOA Accreditation 2025
[Image: GC welcomes ALCUCOA Accreditation 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, November 26, 2025**
The three-day accreditation kicks off as the Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) arrives today, November 26, at the Gordon College (GC) Main Bldg.
This year, the Bachelor of Science in Business Administration (BSBA) Major in Marketing Management vies for Level III Accreditation.
Furthermore, the Bachelor of Science in Accountancy (BSA), Bachelor in Elementary Education (BEEd), Bachelor of Early Childhood Education (BECEd), Bachelor of Secondary Education Major in Filipino (BSEd-Fil), and Bachelor of Secondary Education Major in Science (BSEd-Sci) are aiming for the Level II Accreditation.
Meanwhile, Bachelor of Culture and Arts Education (BCAEd) and Bachelor of Physical Education (BPEd) are now candidates for this year’s accreditation.
The accreditation 2025 is set from November 26-28, at the Function Hall, Gordon College Main Bldg. | via John Patrick Mateo/ The FOREFRONT

# “2,000 change-makers” attend NSTP grad ‘25
[Image: “2,000 change-makers” attend NSTP grad ‘25]
[NSTP](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/nstp/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, June 8, 2025**
Two thousand Gordon College (GC) students enrolled in their National Service and Training Program (NSTP) courses filled the SMX Convention Center, Olongapo City, on Wednesday, for this year’s NSTP Graduation Ceremony.
With the theme, “Volunteerism in Action: Shaping Leaders, Strengthening Community”, this year’s batch of NSTP graduates was graced by Miss Manila 2014 Dr. Krischelle D. Halili.
Dr. Halili emphasized that leadership is an “act not for yourself, but for others” in her address. “Serve something greater than yourself and take your friends with you.”
Moreover, she called on students to love the country and God while also channeling ‘tech natives’ to participate in online community initiatives as well as community services and university council activities on-site.
Leading the initial confirmation of the graduates, Vice President for Academic Affairs Dr. Darwin P. Paguio also delivered his special message, focusing on the impact of genuine leadership.
“True leadership is not defined merely by title or accolades but by the impact you make in the lives of others,” he said as he congratulated the “two thousand change-makers” afterward.
This was followed by the awarding of certificates to Literary Training Service (LTS) and Civic Welfare Training Service (CWTS) completers from the different departments of the institution.
NSTP Instructor Mr. Hubert Lew Magrata delivered his closing remarks to officially close the program.

# 𝐓𝐮𝐥𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐃𝐮𝐧𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐦 (𝐓𝐃𝐏) 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐓𝐞𝐫𝐭𝐢𝐚𝐫𝐲 𝐄𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐒𝐮𝐛𝐬𝐢𝐝𝐲 (𝐓𝐄𝐒) 𝐃𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐛𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧
[Image: 𝐓𝐮𝐥𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐃𝐮𝐧𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐦 (𝐓𝐃𝐏) 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐓𝐞𝐫𝐭𝐢𝐚𝐫𝐲 𝐄𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐒𝐮𝐛𝐬𝐢𝐝𝐲 (𝐓𝐄𝐒) 𝐃𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐛𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧]
**Thursday, December 11, 2025**
𝐀𝐍𝐍𝐎𝐔𝐍𝐂𝐄𝐌𝐄𝐍𝐓
𝐓𝐮𝐥𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐃𝐮𝐧𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐦 (𝐓𝐃𝐏) 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐓𝐞𝐫𝐭𝐢𝐚𝐫𝐲 𝐄𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐒𝐮𝐛𝐬𝐢𝐝𝐲 (𝐓𝐄𝐒) 𝐃𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐛𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧
To all 𝐥𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐝 𝐓𝐃𝐏 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐓𝐄𝐒 𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐞𝐬, please be advised that the claiming of your financial grants will be held at 𝐆𝐨𝐫𝐝𝐨𝐧 𝐂𝐨𝐥𝐥𝐞𝐠𝐞, 𝐅𝐮𝐧𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐇𝐚𝐥𝐥 (𝟑𝐫𝐝 𝐅𝐥𝐨𝐨𝐫) 𝐨𝐧 𝐃𝐞𝐜𝐞𝐦𝐛𝐞𝐫 𝟏𝟏–𝟏𝟐, 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓, 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝟕:𝟎𝟎 𝐀𝐌 𝐭𝐨 𝟓:𝟎𝟎 𝐏𝐌.
This payout covers the following:
• 𝐓𝐃𝐏 𝐁𝐚𝐭𝐜𝐡 𝟕.𝟏 – 𝟐𝐧𝐝 𝐒𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫, 𝐀𝐜𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐜 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟑–𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟒
• 𝐓𝐃𝐏 𝐁𝐚𝐭𝐜𝐡 𝟑 – 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐒𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫, 𝐀𝐜𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐜 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟒–𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓
• 𝐓𝐄𝐒 𝐋𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐧 𝟏𝟓 – 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐒𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫, 𝐀𝐜𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐜 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟒–𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓
[Image: 📄]
𝐑𝐞𝐪𝐮𝐢𝐫𝐞𝐝 𝐃𝐨𝐜𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬
Please prepare the following complete documents :
1. 𝐓𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐩𝐡𝐨𝐭𝐨𝐜𝐨𝐩𝐢𝐞𝐬 of your 𝐬𝐭𝐮𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 𝐨𝐫 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐝 𝐠𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 (𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐡 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞), each with 𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐧 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐬
2. 𝐅𝐨𝐫 𝐀𝐮𝐭𝐡𝐨𝐫𝐢𝐳𝐞𝐝 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐬 (𝐈𝐦𝐦𝐞𝐝𝐢𝐚𝐭𝐞 𝐅𝐚𝐦𝐢𝐥𝐲 𝐎𝐍𝐋𝐘) — all in 𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐜𝐨𝐩𝐢𝐞𝐬:
a. Original 𝐀𝐮𝐭𝐡𝐨𝐫𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐋𝐞𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫 with the grantee’s wet signature.
b. Photocopy of the 𝐬𝐭𝐮𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 𝐨𝐫 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐝 𝐠𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 (with signature) of the grantee, each with 𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐧 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐬.
c. Photocopy of the 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐝 𝐠𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 (with signature) of the authorized representative, each with 𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐧 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐬.
3. 𝐓𝐄𝐒 𝐆𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐞𝐬 𝐎𝐧𝐥𝐲
• For those who have not yet submitted: Please provide your 𝐂𝐞𝐫𝐭𝐢𝐟𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐲 (Purpose: Educational Assistance).
[Image: 📋]
𝐕𝐢𝐞𝐰 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐬𝐜𝐡𝐞𝐝𝐮𝐥𝐞 𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞:
<https://tinyurl.com/PayoutTDP>
<https://tinyurl.com/PayoutTES>
[Image: 🔔]
𝐈𝐦𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐭 𝐍𝐨𝐭𝐞𝐬
1. Please bring 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐞𝐭𝐞 𝐝𝐨𝐜𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 and strictly follow your 𝐚𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐞𝐝 𝐬𝐜𝐡𝐞𝐝𝐮𝐥𝐞 to avoid crowding.
2. Bring 𝐨𝐧𝐞 𝐬𝐞𝐭 of required documents 𝐩𝐞𝐫 𝐬𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫. If you’re included in 𝐛𝐨𝐭𝐡 𝐬𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐬, prepare 𝐭𝐰𝐨 (𝟐) 𝐬𝐞𝐭𝐬 𝐨𝐟 𝐞𝐚𝐜𝐡 𝐝𝐨𝐜𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬.
3. This is 𝐍𝐎𝐓 for application.

# Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Awareness Campaign
[Image: Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Awareness Campaign]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Monday, March 13, 2023**
The successful execution of earthquake and fire prevention simulation exercises is essential for reducing risks and enhancing disaster preparation. These simulations play an essential role in strengthening the resilience of communities and organisations in the face of earthquakes and fires. These sessions were successfully carried out owing to the committed efforts of CESU coordinators, admin/faculty volunteers as facilitators, and our resource speakers, Ms. Jerica Baccay (Head of GC DRRMU) and Mr. Jun Ecleo (GC Safety Officer).

# Fifty Titles Received from Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas
[Image: Fifty Titles Received from Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas]
[LIMC](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/limc/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

The Gordon College Library and Instructional Media Center (LIMC) welcomed the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) in giving them fifty (50) titles of learning resources from their Knowledge Resource Network Collection.   
Mr. Marlon Atencio, College Librarian received the learning resources from the representatives of the BSP.

# THE 4TH GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD: Call for Nomination 2025
[Image: THE 4TH GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD: Call for Nomination 2025]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/)
**Monday, August 4, 2025**
**GENERAL INFORMATION AND GUIDELINES**
Pursuant to Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. (GCAAI) Board Resolution No. 4, series of 2022, the GCAAI establishes the DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD (DASA) to honor Gordon College graduates who have excelled both in their professional and personal careers and who have served as an example to other alumni, students, and the community, both locally and globally. Starting this year, the GCAAI Board of Trustees will recognize the exemplary service and distinguished achievements that brought honor to the Institution and have made significant contributions to society. The GC-DASA recipients will exemplify the core values that the Institution uphold since its establishment: Exceptional Character, Remarkable Excellence, And Unselfish Service.  
The GC-DASA shall be the highest honor bestowed by the Alumni Association and will be an event to be celebrated annually as part of the GC Founding Anniversary and/or GC Alumni Homecoming.  
**GENERAL GUIDELINES:**
1. Only graduates of Gordon College (or GC, formerly Olongapo City Colleges) are eligible for the GC-DASA. The nominee shall be a member of the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. as confirmed by its Board.
2. Any GC graduate, faculty member, or stakeholders may make nominations for the GC-DASA. Self–  
   nominations are accepted. The nominee shall accomplish the Official Nomination Form to be provided by the GCAAI. No limitation will be placed on the total number of nominations accepted for consideration.
3. The nominee shall have no pending administrative or criminal case upon nomination. He/she shall exhibit a high degree of character & integrity.
4. Only one recipient per college/program will be awarded. In case the college/program will have no  
   nominees/recipients, the awarding ceremony will still commence. Likewise, the GCAAI Board of Trustees can directly, without prejudice to the respective college, nominate and evaluate for such an award though the college/program have already a candidate.
5. Only living candidates are considered. In the event of the death of a candidate between notification of the award and the award ceremony, the award may be given posthumously with the permission of the family and the GCAAI. In the event that the awardee declines to accept the award and/or fails to attend the awarding ceremonies, the GCAAI reserves the right to withdraw the award.
6. A GC graduate sitting as a member of the selection committee or a member of the GCAAI Board of Trustees is not eligible to receive the award. This individual is eligible for nomination after a one-year waiting period from the date he/she steps down from his/her role.
7. Recipients are only eligible for one GC-DASA in a lifetime.
8. The recipients will receive a plaque or certificate of recognition and will be posted on the official FB page of the GCAAI. It will announce the annual recipients through various methods and honor each recipient with the appropriate recognition.
9. If for any reason, the GCAAI is not able to conduct the Awards as planned (including due to tampering, unauthorized intervention, fraud, technical difficulties, pandemic, or any other causes beyond the control of the organizer which impact the administration, security, fairness, integrity or proper conduct of the Awards), then they reserve the right in their sole discretion to make any reasonable variation to the Awarding Ceremony.
10. The Nomination Committee and/or GCAAI Board of Trustees may disqualify any nominee that they  
    reasonably determine has breached any of the terms and conditions or otherwise engaged in unlawful or other improper conduct that may bring the Awards into disrepute or jeopardize the fair and proper conduct of the Awards.
11. Other guidelines may be promulgated as decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees.
**NOMINATION COMMITTEE:**  
The Nomination Committee (NC) may be organized by the Dean/OIC in every college and/or the GCAAI. The committee must be composed of at least three (3) members from their respective colleges. The total count of committee members should be an odd number. The chairperson of the NC shall be the Dean or its duly authorized representative and for the GCAAI, any member can initiate such nomination. No member of the NC must be related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the nominees/candidates for awards. If the Dean/OIC is related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the candidates for awards, the Dean/OIC must inhibit him/herself from participating in the process. The approval shall come from the person next in rank.  
The NC shall:
1. Abide by the criteria set forth by the GCAAI.
2. For nominees endorsed by the College, it shall issue a certificate of authenticity to all the documents that will be submitted to the GCAAI.
3. Deliberate on the qualifications of the nominees for the awards based on the criteria and the documents submitted.
4. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the result of nomination for approval.
5. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the resolution on any related issue that may arise from the results of the awards.
6. Ensure that the guidelines stipulated herein are followed.
**NOMINATION AND SELECTION PROCESS:**
1. Complete the online/hard copy Nomination Form.
2. Nominators will receive an auto-generated copy via email of what they submitted, i.e., the nomination form including any attachments. In any case, it is not available, the NC may determine what means of notification it will conduct to the nominators to confirm the submission of the nomination form and its attachments.
3. Nominees and nominators will be contacted by the NC/GCAAI within three days of receipt of the  
   nomination to acknowledge receipt.
4. A nominee may be asked to clarify or submit further information and evidence to assist the NC/GCAAI in assessing the nomination at any stage of the selection process.
5. The NC/GCAAI may also seek verification of the nominee’s achievements from their current or previous employer or other relevant individual or organization representative. By being nominated for this Award, the nominees are giving consent to the NC/GCAAI to contact these individuals to verify nominations.
6. Nominations will be reviewed and shortlisted by the NC/GCAAI and nominees who have not been  
   shortlisted will be notified. The GC-DASA candidates will be asked to submit a brief profile for this purpose. The candidate shall garner a total score of 60 points to be included in the shortlist. If a nominee cannot meet the required points, he/she is automatically excluded from the shortlist.
7. Every member of the NC will recommend/endorse the final candidate to the GCAAI President and then, the GCAAI Board of Trustees, sitting as Awards Selection Committee, will issue a confirmation through a Board Resolution. The report on the results of the NC shall be signed by all members of the committee and certified by the chairperson.
8. In any case that such protest will be filed during the confirmation of the final candidates, the final decision on the protest shall be decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees. The GCAAI Board of Trustees’ decision is final on all matters relating to the nomination process and the selection of award recipients, and they are not required to provide feedback as to why a nomination was unsuccessful.
Call for Nominations: 4th Distinguished Alumni Service Award (DASA)
The Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. proudly announces the opening of nominations for the 4th Distinguished Alumni Service Award (DASA), which will run from August 4 to 22, 2025. This prestigious recognition honors outstanding alumni who have demonstrated exceptional service, leadership, and commitment to their profession, community, or alma mater. We encourage all alumni, faculty, and stakeholders to submit nominations for individuals who embody the values and excellence upheld by Gordon College. For complete nomination guidelines, kindly scan the QR code below.
The awarding ceremony will be held on October 11, 2025. Let us come together to recognize and celebrate the achievements of Gordon College alumni who continue to make a lasting difference.

# 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐥𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐩𝐚𝐭 𝐧𝐠 𝐤𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐬𝐚 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐨, 𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥 𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐬𝐚𝐤𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐧
[Image: 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐥𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐩𝐚𝐭 𝐧𝐠 𝐤𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐬𝐚 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐨, 𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥 𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐬𝐚𝐤𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐧]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, July 18, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE — Isinalin na ang mga posisyon at kapangyarihan sa mga bagong opisyales, gayundin ang pagpapakilala sa mga bagong kakatawan sa mga GCians sa katatapos na Supreme Student Council Turnover Ceremony na ginanap sa PE Hall nitong ika-14 ng Hulyo.
Nasaksihan din ng mga lider-estudyante mula sa iba’t ibang organisasyon sa loob ng institusyon ang nabanggit na seremonya.
Bilang panimula, binigyang diin ng noo’y SSC Chairperson Frances Daphne Mobo ang seremonya sa paglalahad ng Budget at Accomplishment Reports ng kanyang termino.
Samantala, inamin ni Mobo na nabigo nilang tuparin ang ilang mga pangakong proyekto para sa mga mag-aaral.
“Failures make us better individuals. Leadership teaches you how to be accountable [for] your own actions and how to accept constructive criticism. To our student leaders present here today, do remember that leadership is not about positions or titles because those are temporary. Leadership is about passion and dedication,” aniya.
Bukod dito, binigyan ng pagkakataon ang bagong termino upang talakayin at bigyang linaw ang kanilang mga inihandang proyekto at aktibidades sa harap ng kanilang mga kapwa lider-estudyante.
Sumatutal, mayroong 12 na proyekto at 17 na aktibidades na gustong ilunsad ang naturang termino sa pangunguna ni ngayo’y SSC Chairperson Julier Samonte. Ilan dito ay ang GCLAMP 2.0: Enhancing Access in the GCLAMP, CODE 200: Tech Industry Partnership Found, BIN-novation: Classroom for a Cleaner Future para sa kanilang proyekto, samantala ay inahain naman ang Student First Starter Packs: College Life Unboxed, Think Green: Green Revolutions for GCians, Beyond the Myths: Understanding HIV/AIDS & Prevention para sa kanilang mga aktibidades.
Ngunit ang lahat ng kanilang programa ay pawang mga panukala pa lamang at posibleng mayroong ibang hindi mapayagang mailunsad, paglilinaw ng bagong konseho.
Bilang karagdagan sa mga pangako ni Samonte, bagong kinatawan ng konseho, nais niya at ng kaniyang konseho na palawakin ang komunikasyon at transparency sa pagsasagawa ng regular student consultations, open forums, at online suggestion platforms para marinig ang tunay na saloobin ng mga estudyante. Kasama rin dito ang paglabas ng general plan of actions, budget breakdowns, at accomplishment reports upang mas mapanatili ang tiwala ng bawat estudyante sa konseho.
“Pangatlo, mas makabuluhang events para sa lahat. Gagawin nating inclusive at meaningful ang bawat SSC-led event. Hindi lang ito basta kasiyahan dahil gagawa rin po tayo ng mga aktibidad na magtataguyod ng leadership, advocacy, at student development.”
“Pang apat, pagpapalakas ng bawat student organization sa bawat departamento. Bilang inyong chairperson, sisiguraduhin kong makakaramdam ng buong suporta ang bawat departamental organization,” dagdag pa niya.
Paalala naman ng bagong SSC Chairperson na ang kaniyang pagkapanalo noong nakaraang eleksyon ay hindi lamang tagumpay ng isang tao kundi tagumpay rin ng bawat estudyante na naniniwala na pwedeng marinig, makilahok, at maging bahagi ng pagbabago.
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# Ex-NSO prexy secures rank 9 spot; GC among top schools in Feb 2026 PNLE
[Image: Ex-NSO prexy secures rank 9 spot; GC among top schools in Feb 2026 PNLE]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, March 6, 2026**
Alumna Alexis Molon Penuliar of the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) ranked ninth in the February 2026 Philippine Nursing Licensure Examination (PNLE), as announced by the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC) today, March 6.
Graduated cum laude, the former Gordon College – Nursing Student Organization (GC-NSO) president earned an 89.80% rating in the recent board examination.
The institution recorded 55 passers out of 60 total takers, posting an overall passing rate of 91.67%. Comprising this are 55 out of 57 first-time passers while none of the three repeat examinees passed.
Moreover, GC was among the country’s top-performing schools at 4th rank in the recent licensure exam with 50 to 99 examinees and at least 85% passing percentage, far surpassing the national passing rate of 44.24%.
Meanwhile, Alyanna Marie Ramos Villegas of Saint Paul University – Tuguegarao (SPU-T) was the February 2026 PNLE’s Top 1 Passer, having 92.00% rating. SPU-T also emerged as the Top 1 Performing School with 100 or more examinees, having 99.10% passing rate with 220 successful examinees out of 222.
PRC administered the examination on February 26 and 27 in various testing centers, including Manila, Baguio, Cebu, Davao, and several key provinces such as Palawan, Laguna, and Batangas. | via Alyssa Marie Alcantara and Keane Rafael Abdon/The FOREFRONT

# EASE ON SCHOOL UNIFORMPOLICY DUE TO EXTREMEHEAT CONDITIONS (EXTENSION)

[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/)
**Saturday, May 4, 2024**
𝙀𝙮𝙚𝙨 𝙝𝙚𝙧𝙚, 𝙂𝘾𝙞𝙖𝙣𝙨!

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/campus-life/library-and-instructional-media-center/limc-services

# LIMC Services
## **READER’S / CIRCULATION SERVICES**
Readers/circulation services are provided to keep the library patrons in the use of information resources and facilities. It includes services such as lending and retrieving of books, assisting in literature search, locating books, shelving, recalling books that were checked out.
**Function**
1. To serve promptly and efficiently all library patrons.
2. To substitute when possible, suitable and adequate materials if the desired materials is not available or charged out.
3. To assist students and other library patrons in using the card catalog – OPAC and teach them the importance as the index to the collection of books in the library.
4. To become acquainted with the interests, problems, capacities and needs of the students and faculty.
5. To provide efficient service.
## **CIRCULATION PROCESS**
Circulation is the primary task of the libraries. Its objective is to maximize the utilization of all materials in order to serve a great number of library users. To achieve this, an effective charging system is established.
**Activities**
1. Require all students who are officially enrolled to present their school ID.
2. Check in and check out books and other information materials.
3. Assist readers in the use of the library catalog –OPAC and print/card, and explain the arrangement of books on the shelves.
4. File and maintain patron’s record.
5. Keep statistical records to provide a basis for understanding, planning and carrying out the circulation job in terms of users’ demand.
6. Assist/help students/users in the selection of books and other materials for reading.
7. Control the material collection so that the availability of materials to the patron is maximized.
8. Shelf-read books in stacks to ensure proper placement and order in shelving.
## **CHARGING/ DISCHARGING SERVICES**
A library, no matter how it is, must adopt its own system in charging and discharging library materials in order to record and trace the borrowers of the book
**Procedure**
1. In borrowing a book, one presents his or her school ID to the librarian and asks for the book/s he or she wishes to borrow.
2. The borrower signs the library card and book card attached to the book.
3. The library card and the book card are clipped together and filed in the charging box alphabetically.
4. The staff in charge will log details of the borrowed book/s in the log book.
5. When a book is returned, the book card and the library card are pulled out from the file of loaned-out books. The borrower card will give back to the student with a remark of returned and the date when the book is returned.
6. Put a remarks on a book card when the book is return.
7. Return the book card into the book pocket and return it to the shelves.
## **REFERENCE BOOK SERVICE**
Reference service handles inquiry and assists user in find the information they require, access it, and use it to meet their needs. According to Kumar (2003), reference service helps to establish contact between a user and the right document at the right time, thereby saving the time of the user.
**A. Objective of the Reference:**  
The main objective of reference service is to provide the library patrons access to the information resources. The reference work is directly concerned with assistance to users’ information and in using the resources in their study and research. To achieve this objective, the librarian provides the information promptly and efficiently necessary to satisfy the patron’s request.
**Activities**
1. Conduct reference interviews to understand and determine the needs of the clientele for quick sighting of information.
2. Give information and answer factual questions.
3. Help the patron find the needed information and provide guidance in the choice of materials.
4. Give formal and informal instruction on the tools of the library catalog – OPAC, indexes and others.
5. Answer reference questions requiring normal and extended searching.
6. Retrieve reference questions handled for possible future solutions.
7. Guide students in their interest with books and teach them how to love books and to make books an important part of their life;
8. Retrieve reference statistics.
9. Maintain information files.
**B****. Use of OPAC**
1. The student types the subject he/she wants to research.
2. Click SEARCH and jot down the information about the book.
3. Don’t click other keys so as not to destroy the system.
## **LIBRARY ORIENTATION AND INSTRUCTION**
Library orientation refers to helping students feel aware of and comfortable with the facilities and the services available, with the location of collections, and with the people who service those collections. At every start of the school year, the library staff, in coordination with the office of the student welfare and services, give orientation to the freshmen and transferees during the general orientation program.
**Objectives:**
1. To stimulate interest in the use of library information resource.
2. To maximize the use of references and other materials for their research work and study.
3. To help them instill the habit of self-education through books and other materials.
**Activities**
1. Give class orientation to all new students. This orientation includes the following:  
   – introduction of library personnel  
   – library sections  
   – library services  
   – arrangement of books and other materials in the library  
   – rules and regulations  
   – classification system used  
   – library catalog  
   – OPAC: its importance and use
2. Bring the students to the library for familiarization of its various sections and resources.
## **PHOTOCOPYING SERVICES**
Policies in Photocopying of Materials:
1. Photocopying of complete books and theses or dissertations is not allowed. Only the abstract of the theses or dissertations may be photocopied.
2. Users are responsible for complying with the copyright act/and intellectual property rights.
3. For materials to be photocopied outside the library, the client should fill out the library card and submit them together with their school ID to the librarian in-charge. (This is only allowed when the photocopy service is not available inside the library).
## **INTERLIBRARY LOAN/ REFERRAL SERVICES**
Procedure:
1. The student is advised to exhaust first our resources before going out to other libraries.
2. If the references or the materials are not available, the student is advised to fill out a request form for a referral letter.
3. The librarian will issue a referral letter and give instruction or advise the student what to do in other libraries before she signs.
## **INTERNET SERVICES**
This section is equipped with computers to gain access to information on the Internet. Faculty and students can use this section to search for materials available in the library using subscribed and free online databases.
Procedure:
1. Approach the library staff for the availability of computer units.
2. The library follows the “first come, first served” policy.
3. Sign into the Computer Usage Form
4. Proceed the computer unit, the library staff will set the time for you.
5. The library limits the time a client can use the computer to one (1) hour (but can exceed if there are no other users waiting) to maximize its utilization and serve many students.
## **BULLETIN BOARD DISPLAY**
Activities:
1. The librarian makes a complete list of all newly acquired materials.
2. The theme of the display for the bulletin board is determined.
3. The librarian plans the design of the display.
4. The librarian gathers all the needed materials for the display.
5. The librarian prepares and displays the material in the bulletin board.
Priorities and Guidelines in the Display:
* Priority is given to the library who owns the bulletin board.
* Notices and publications will be under the prerogative of the Head Librarian using the best interest to the Gordon College community as his reference point.  
  The following types of information will not be allowed for display:
  + Posters promoting political parties advocating the election of any candidate or a stand in any issue concerning elections.
  + Notices of merchandise or services for sale
  + Rental announcements
  + The appearance and content of the notice must be acceptable or suitable for display.
  + Generally, notices will not be posted for longer than three (3) weeks

# 𝘼 𝙇𝙚𝙜𝙖𝙘𝙮 𝙤𝙛 𝙀𝙭𝙘𝙚𝙡𝙡𝙚𝙣𝙘𝙚, 𝘼 𝙁𝙪𝙩𝙪𝙧𝙚 𝙤𝙛 𝙄𝙢𝙥𝙖𝙘𝙩
[Image: 𝘼 𝙇𝙚𝙜𝙖𝙘𝙮 𝙤𝙛 𝙀𝙭𝙘𝙚𝙡𝙡𝙚𝙣𝙘𝙚, 𝘼 𝙁𝙪𝙩𝙪𝙧𝙚 𝙤𝙛 𝙄𝙢𝙥𝙖𝙘𝙩]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, August 17, 2025**
Gordon College is proud to announce that the Philippine Society of Customs Administration Students (PSCAS) Gordon College Chapter soared to new heights at the PSCAS National Awards on August 16, 2025, at Wind Residences, Tagaytay City!
PSCAS GC Chapter was honored with the most prestigious award: Top Performing PSCAS Chapter!
This incredible achievement is a testament to our dedication to excellence and unwavering commitment to our community.
We also brought home a constellation of awards, including Most Outstanding in:
\* Community Impact & Civic Engagement
\* Leadership & Institutional Development Projects
\* Specialized Customs Training & Webinar Programs
And we were recognized as Outstanding in:
\* Academic Excellence & Professional Development
\* Career Preparation & Skills Development
\* Culture, Arts & Wellness Promotion
\* Environmental Advocacy & Sustainability
\* Student Engagement & Community Building Initiatives
\* Student Support & Mentorship Initiatives
We want to give a special shout-out to two of our very own who stood out among the best students in the country:
\* Kris Rean Agaton was named the Top 1 Most Outstanding BSCA Student of the Philippines 2025.
\* Renmmelle Kyle Cerilles was named among the top 3 most outstanding BSCA Students in the Philippines in 2025.
Your hard work, leadership, and passion have made a mark, and we couldn’t be prouder of your achievements.
This incredible success belongs to every member, officer, and adviser who contributed their time and talent to make our chapter shine.
Thank you for your tireless efforts!

# Blessing of Gordon College Annex Building
[Image: Blessing of Gordon College Annex Building]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, August 11, 2025**
Pormal na binasbasan ang bagong Annex Building ng Gordon College ngayong Agosto 11, 2025, sa pangunguna ni Rev. Fr. Roque Villanueva.
Dumalo sa makabuluhang seremonya sina Mayor Atty. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., BCDA Director Rolen C. Paulino, Gordon College President Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, mga Konsehal ng Lungsod, at ilan pang opisyal, kasama ang mga estudyante, guro, at kawani ng Gordon College bilang pagsuporta sa makasaysayang pagbubukas ng bagong gusali.
Mahigit sa tatlong libong karagdagang estudyante ang makikinabang sa bagong gusali.
Itinakda ang pagbubukas ng Annex Building kasabay ng pasimula ng klase ngayong Academic Year 2025–2026, bilang bahagi ng patuloy na pagtugon ng lokal na pamahalaan at pamunuan ng kolehiyo sa lumalaking pangangailangan para sa dekalidad at makabagong pasilidad sa edukasyon.
Courtesy to [**Olongapo City Information Center**](https://www.facebook.com/olongapopublicaffairs?__cft__[0]=AZUoEEuU7JRBp8m9YZ_lMtmfx9IJRxP901v3h3T0hfeZn1kxjLV0Z1r6Jfs3Bag4ifapazsLBegn1V1TQu1yB6pyZZx5ZTFUaZUlZqA2TOCdxP8_aqb4eGNYK49-vrS4sSl1XTuYTylL_EcYgARMMYYnCv7KUeZIwb2Y985F8OVk2jWSh389TouCTzffLa23nDTYIT4g6th1iQvxaJMV5SncNFYJ5OXzmrj-OwOukqkGvw&__tn__=-UC%2CP-y-R). Read full article [here](https://www.facebook.com/olongapopublicaffairs/posts/pfbid02Bss2CRG99Jb9rXr2nYMB6CQ7p5cWsYinmTc1Lh32eBMQ1V73JH1xNo6fxvSiRV7gl?__cft__[0]=AZUoEEuU7JRBp8m9YZ_lMtmfx9IJRxP901v3h3T0hfeZn1kxjLV0Z1r6Jfs3Bag4ifapazsLBegn1V1TQu1yB6pyZZx5ZTFUaZUlZqA2TOCdxP8_aqb4eGNYK49-vrS4sSl1XTuYTylL_EcYgARMMYYnCv7KUeZIwb2Y985F8OVk2jWSh389TouCTzffLa23nDTYIT4g6th1iQvxaJMV5SncNFYJ5OXzmrj-OwOukqkGvw&__tn__=%2CO%2CP-y-R).

# Gordon College Launches State-of-the-Art Mac Laboratory
[Image: Gordon College Launches State-of-the-Art Mac Laboratory]
[CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, April 28, 2025**
Gordon College (GC) proudly announces the official launch of its new Mac Laboratory, a significant enhancement to the technological resources available to its students. The laboratory is equipped with 25 advanced iMac M4 units, representing a substantial investment in providing students with industry-standard technology to support their academic pursuits and skill development.
GC extends its deepest gratitude to the City Mayor of Olongapo, the Honorable Rolen C. Paulino Jr., for his steadfast commitment to the betterment of the College and his invaluable support for this initiative.
“The launch of the Mac Laboratory is a momentous occasion for Gordon College, made possible through the collective vision and support of our esteemed City Mayor, dedicated officials, and valued stakeholders,” stated Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, College President. “These state-of-the-art iMac M4 units will empower our students with the technological proficiency demanded by today’s industries, further solidifying our commitment to providing a world-class education.”
This initiative was also made possible through the strong support of Hon. Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino, Chairperson of the Committee on Education, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, College President, Dr. Erlinda S. Casela-Abarintos, Dean of the College of Computer Studies and the Vice President for Administration and Finance, Dr. Darwin P. Paguio, Vice President for Academic Affairs, College Officials, and other stakeholders. This advancement underscores GC’s commitment to providing cutting-edge resources for its students.
Together, we will strive for more innovations for the college, empowering our students and faculty to excel in the ever-evolving landscape of technology and design.

# Rights and Obligations of GCAAI Members
#### Rights of Members
All members, regular and honorary, who complied with all the requirements, guidelines, and policies set forth by the Association are members in good standing.
Every member in good standing shall have the following rights:
1. To attend the regular and special meetings of the general membership of the Association;
2. To vote and be elected into office;
3. To propose amendments and resolutions, and to vote on those presented at the meetings above mentioned;
4. To request access to official records, documents, and papers pertaining to the acts, transactions, or decisions of the Association unless otherwise specified;
5. To use the properties and facilities of the Association according to the rules and regulations agreed upon for this purpose;
6. Such other rights and privileges as may be granted by the Board of Trustees.
#### Article 4 Section 5 – The right to vote and be elected as Trustees
The right to vote and be elected as Trustees of this Association shall be exclusively granted to Active Members and members in good standing. Provided, they are listed in the SEC Membership Book/and other forms of registration conducted by the Association and shall be certified correct by the Corporate Secretary. Provided, further, that only active members shall be appointed to any position in the Association.
#### Article 4 Section 6
It is understood that those who have been accepted members of the Association prior to the effectivity of the present By-Laws shall continue to be active members. Provided, further, that after the first general assembly and election, they shall satisfy the requirements stipulated under Section 3.
#### Duties and Obligations of Members
1. Every member, whether regular or honorary, has the responsibility to abide in this Constitution and Bylaws, and rules and regulations set forth by the GC-AA.
2. Every member must pay membership fees and annual fees in an amount determined by the majority vote of the GC-AA Executive Committee and Board. All dues payments shall be made to the treasurer and/or duly representative of the Association.
3. Every member must support and promote the thrusts and objectives of the GC-AA and the College.
4. Every member must participate actively in all Gordon College activities and GC-AA programs and projects.
5. Every member must exercise his/her rights and do his/her responsibilities as a GC-AA member.
6. Every member shall be entitled to exercise full membership rights, including the right to vote and hold office within the Association in accordance with the By-laws.
#### Disciplinary Action Against Members
The GC-AA Board of Trustees shall review and act upon all disputes regarding infractions of Association rules and regulations. The Trustees can form an Ad Hoc Committee that can render decisions in terminating a member from the Association.
The final decision of the said committee shall become final upon the approval of the GC-AA Board of Trustees at its regular monthly or special meeting. The GC-AA Board may overrule the Committee by a two-thirds (2/3) vote.

  * [GCAAI Seal](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gcaai/gcaai-seal/)
  * [Rights and Obligations of GCAAI Members](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gcaai/rights-and-obligations-of-gcaai-members/)

# GC Warriors Make a Splash in 2025 ALCU R3 Cultural and Sports Competition
[Image: GC Warriors Make a Splash in 2025 ALCU R3 Cultural and Sports Competition]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, April 30, 2025**
To our incredible Gordon College Warriors, your hard work, resilience, and unwavering dedication throughout the 2025 Association of Local Colleges and Universities Region III Sports and Cultural Competition (ALCU R3) have been nothing short of inspiring.
Thank you for being shining examples of sportsmanship, perseverance, and true school spirit. You represented Gordon College with heart, honor, and pride, and now, your efforts have been rewarded with well-deserved recognition:
Gordon College proudly achieved:
Sports Competition – 1st Runner-Up Overall
Cultural Competition – 2nd Runner-Up Overall
Our deepest gratitude to Hon. Rolen C. Paulino, City Mayor; Hon. Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino, Committee on Education Chairperson; Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, College President; Ms. Lovelyn P. Ceralde, Vice President for Student Welfare and Services; College Officials, Coaches, and all other personnel for their unwavering support in this endeavor.
Continue to rise, inspire, and conquer. The entire GC community is incredibly proud of you!

# GC marks a milestone in ALCUCOA 2025
[Image: GC marks a milestone in ALCUCOA 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, December 3, 2025**
The three-day program comes to an end as Gordon College (GC) conducts an exit conference for the Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) last November 28, at the Gordon College Main Building, Function Hall.
Vying for one Level III program, five Level II programs, and two candidate programs this year, the institution’s big step is a leap for development, embodying character, excellence, and service along the way.
“This is not the end,” Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano opened the exit conference of this year’s accreditation with a firm statement.
The accreditors commended the institution’s strengths including the Library Ambassadors Organization (LAO), community extension programs, and professionalism.
Meanwhile, they also recommended improvements such as stronger programs to supplement both students’ and teachers’ needs, more tangible and sustainable community extensions, and subjects that focus on application rather than theories.
As Dr. Elizabeth Montero, the supervising accreditor said in her message, “The new oil now is consistency,” a reminder to stay true and stand into the lenses of continuous development where growth is evident and seen.
In response, Ms. Geraldine Raydanas and Mrs. Rowenalyn Sagun expressed their utmost gratitude and promised to improve in the areas mentioned.
Ending the program, Dr. Kristin Joy Mendoza, secretary of GC’s Board of Trustees, left a valuable statement regarding institutionalization and sustainability.
“Every accreditation is an opportunity to innovate and fill the spaces where we can do better,” Dr. Mendoza emphasized.
Meanwhile, the results of the 2025 accreditation are set to be announced in the next few weeks. | via Mikhaela Sagun/The FOREFRONT

# Fostering Mental Health
[Image: Fostering Mental Health]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Wednesday, July 26, 2023**
In adherence to our commitment to community service, the GC Community Extension Services Unit (GC-CESU), in collaboration with the GC Guidance Office and Tapinac Senior High School, successfully organized and conducted a seminar on July 31, 2023. The seminar aimed to enhance the resilience of the attendees by equipping them with techniques to address and manage challenges, emphasizing the importance of self-care, addressing mental health stigma, and facilitating access to mental health services. Ms. Mechel Bautista, GC Guidance Office Head, served as the resource speaker. The seminar saw active participation from 42 teachers from Tapinac Senior High School and took place at the Gordon College function hall.

# GC presents one thousand students in graduation rites
[Image: GC presents one thousand students in graduation rites]
[Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, July 10, 2025**
Gordon College’s (GC) 23rd Commencement Exercises saw a total of 1,016 passionate graduates walk in the aisle of Subic Bay Exhibition and Convention Center (SBECC) this year, donning their respective togas and sablays.
Amidst heavy rains, Cluster I filled the morning ceremony with 487 graduates from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) and Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM).
Meanwhile, 529 students from Cluster II’s College of Allied Health Sciences (CAHS), College of Computer Studies (CCS), and College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) graced the stage during the afternoon rites.
The graduates are products of Free Higher Education (FHE) from the institution and taxpayers, who enjoyed it during their tenure.

# Enhancing Reading Literacy Training
[Image: Enhancing Reading Literacy Training]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Saturday, June 24, 2023**
A successful whole-day training was conducted through a collaborative effort involving the GC CESU, Academic Affairs, CEAS immersion instructors, and dedicated volunteers from the DepEd teaching staff. The training’s success can be attributed to the contributions of Mr. Willy R. Antigo, Ms. Enggelyn Yandoc, Ms. Meryl Bustos, and Ms. Mara Grace Inocencio, who served as resource trainers and played crucial roles in its smooth execution. The training saw enthusiastic participation from a diverse group of student facilitators, representing ten different programs within the College of Education, Arts, and Sciences. There were 117 trainees in the morning session and 117 in the afternoon session.

# GCAAI Membership
#### Qualifications for Membership
A. The qualifications for membership to the Association are either:
1. Holder of academic degree from Gordon College (formerly Olongapo City Colleges)
2. Holder of secondary/basic education diploma from Gordon College-Senior High School
3. Holder of academic title or postgraduate diploma from Gordon College (formerly Olongapo City Colleges)
4. Not having been dismissed for misconduct/offenses or scholastic/academic deficiency which is automatically considered not a member of the Association.
B. Membership to the association may be bestowed upon the approval of the GC-AA Board of Trustees:
1. Holders of honorary degrees from the College;
2. Former or current members of the faculty and staff of the College who have rendered service to the College for at least ten (10) years.
C. Active members are those who have qualified as members under Section 1 and 2, and are either:
1. Members who have paid lifetime membership dues;
2. Members who have paid and are up-to-date in their annual membership dues;
3. Members who have participated in such other programs that sustain, develop, and promote the Association’s objectives as determined by the Board.
D. The Alumni Oath shall be recited by a member who signifies its willingness to be part of and reflect its allegiance to the Association. The Alumni Oath shall be administered by the President or its duly authorized representative in a ceremony, commencement and/or event. The Alumni Oath in its current form is as follows:
*I **(state your name)**,*  
*a graduate of Gordon College,*  
*hereby undertake to*  
*vigilantly upholds the ideals*  
*and shield the honor of my Alma Mater,*  
*and as a fulfilled member of the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc.,*  
*will uphold its Constitution and By-Laws,*  
*to remain a true paragon towards excellence,*  
*committed to do justice to everyone*  
*in service to our country and our God.*  
*So help me, God!*

# The celebration of arts and culture ignites a fiery PASE Community Concert this 27th Founding Anniversary
[Image: The celebration of arts and culture ignites a fiery PASE Community Concert this 27th Founding Anniversary]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, February 25, 2026**
The Performing Arts Student Ensemble (PASE) ignites a synergy of performances on the second day of the 27th Founding Anniversary on February 25, at East Tapinac Oval Grounds.
The first segment featured PASE Ballo’s contemporary dance performance to the song ‘Tala,’ touching the hearts of GCians with their moving steps.
Furthermore, PASE Voci’s chorale performance filled the air with harmonic voices and unity, creating an ethereal atmosphere through their cover of ‘Bagong Umaga’ by Bayang Barrios.
The third segment was a play by PASE Teatro, showcasing relatable acting that highlighted the reality of social media’s impact on people’s lives.
In addition, PASE Ballo returned for another performance of a cultural inspired dance filled with enthusiasm and energy.
Moreover, the fifth segment was a collaboration between all PASE ensembles — Cuerdas, Teatro, and Voci. Storytelling and music united on a narrative of Overseas Filipino Workers (OFWs) acted by Teatro and set to Voci and Cuerdas’s rendition of ‘Pinoy Ako’ by Bamboo.
Following that is a performance from Teatro’s newest troupe, the collectives. The four-member rap group sang Filipino hip-hop songs that captured the culture of Pinoy music history. They performed covers of ‘Venus’ by Ron Henely, ‘Ikaw lang’ by Kiyo, and ‘Upuan’ by Gloc 9, bringing back nostalgic vibes to many GCians.
Finally, the last segment featured a collaboration of Voci and Cuerdas. Following this is PASE Ballo’s dance performance, celebrating Olongapo’s culture and the jeepney tradition. The performance transitioned to Cuerdas and Voci, who performed a reggae song.
The event proudly celebrated National Arts Month this February, showcasing the diverse talents and artistry of GCians. Through various performances, the students highlighted their creativity and passion for the arts, demonstrating the vibrant cultural landscape of the institution. It was a testament to the rich artistic expression and talent that GCians have, making it a memorable celebration of arts and culture, leaving a lasting impression on the audiences at the 27th founding anniversary. | via Lei Ydah Lasala Ledesma / The FOREFRONT
Photos by Franz Feria & Lauren Louise Sanita

# Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy
[Image: Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy]
**Friday, August 8, 2025**
Instructions for Gordon College Students: Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy
Purpose:
To foster a clean and respectful campus environment while promoting sustainability through personal responsibility for waste.
Before Class or Activity:
1. Prepare Your Materials:
– Bring Your Own Trash: Carry a small, reusable bag or container to collect any waste you generate during class or activities. This could be a sturdy tote, a lunch bag, or any suitable container.
– Plan for Snacks/Drinks: If you plan to consume food or beverages, pack them in a way that minimizes waste (e.g., reusable containers, bottles).
During Class or Activity:
2. Maintain Your Space:
– Keep Your Area Clean: Throughout the session, stay vigilant about your surroundings. Avoid leaving papers, wrappers, or any personal items strewn about.
– Immediate Clean-Up: If you create a mess (accidental spills, paper cuts, etc.), address it promptly. If cleaning supplies are available, use them to tidy up immediately.
3. Responsible Waste Management:
– Use Your Reusable Container: Whenever you have waste, place it in your reusable bag or container. Keep it nearby for easy access.
– Respect Shared Spaces: Be mindful of not just your space but also those around you. Dispose of any common area waste (e.g., napkins from community snacks) in your own trash.
After Class or Activity:
4. Final Cleanup:
– Inspect Your Area:
Before leaving, take a moment to ensure your seating area and any communal spaces (tables, chairs) are tidy.
– Empty Your Trash into Appropriate Bins: When you leave the classroom or activity, empty your reusable container at designated trash or recycling bins outside the classroom (if available). Take anything that belongs to you with you.
5. Dispose Responsibly:
– Recycle When Possible: If your waste is recyclable, place it in the recycling bin. Be sure to check the college’s recycling guidelines to ensure proper disposal.
Additional Responsibilities:
6. Encourage a Culture of Cleanliness:
– Remind Peers:If you notice a classmate leaving a mess, gently remind them or offer to help.
– Lead by Example: Demonstrating your commitment to the “Clean As You Go” policy can inspire your peers to do the same.
7. Feedback and Improvement:
– Participate in discussions or feedback sessions organized by the college to share your insights on how the Clean As You Go policy is working.
8. Engage with Initiatives:
– Stay updated on any college-led environmental initiatives or clean-up days, and participate actively when you can.
By adhering to these instructions, you contribute to creating a cleaner, more sustainable campus environment while developing valuable habits of personal responsibility and community stewardship. Thank you for doing your part!  
Check the complete details of this policy [here](https://www.facebook.com/GordonCollegeOfficial/videos/785726823793572/?__cft__[0]=AZXuJiWVvZ7O356tbNsKDWGIEwBHPMOrOE8pJ5U5-RPYBTqRlFdOrFHJVI7tXPYSyEQzJdthbkZkDP69fmcOj5KKZ9zFYh5Mfg1O2rxEadaLpJktKNeGky1Ra8F4yFiqVkeH6uDT-vQry2mGACVLcSjmZxVASMT6QKjA_v9L1f09FKEfq4FWhBIbvKNU6ANR3gRcFhjS7VfgYlPdaB5MSnL1&__tn__=%2CO-R).

# Implementation of the School Uniform Policy
[Image: Implementation of the School Uniform Policy]
**Wednesday, July 30, 2025**
𝐀𝐃𝐕𝐈𝐒𝐎𝐑𝐘
The implementation of the school uniform policy will begin on 𝐒𝐞𝐩𝐭𝐞𝐦𝐛𝐞𝐫 𝟖, 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓, 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐅𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐡𝐦𝐞𝐧 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐓𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐟𝐞𝐫𝐞𝐞𝐬, while 𝐒𝐨𝐩𝐡𝐨𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐒𝐞𝐧𝐢𝐨𝐫𝐬 𝐰𝐢𝐥𝐥 𝐛𝐞 𝐫𝐞𝐪𝐮𝐢𝐫𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐰𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐢𝐫 𝐬𝐜𝐡𝐨𝐨𝐥 𝐮𝐧𝐢𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐬 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐀𝐮𝐠𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝟏𝟖, 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓.
𝐖𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐒𝐭𝐮𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 must accomplish their Uniform Exemption Pass from 𝐀𝐮𝐠𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝟏 𝐭𝐨 𝟏𝟓, 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓 𝐚𝐭 𝐫𝐨𝐨𝐦 𝟏𝟐𝟐 at the office of our Student Discipline Officers.
The following shall 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐛𝐞 𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐨𝐰𝐞𝐝:
1. Sleeveless tops, spaghetti straps, razor backs, tubes, hanging or mid rib blouse
2. All types of shorts (walking shorts, cycling shorts, tokong) tight fitting (leggings) and torn or mutilated jeans
3. Clothes with frayed hems
4. Blouses and dresses with plunging necklines/backless
5. Garments that are backless and See-through attires
6. Miniskirts (more than 2 inches above the knee) except where curriculum requires.
7. Long Skirts with high slit
8. Jogging pants (allowed only for PE schedule)
9. Slippers (exemptions: medical reason, weather conditions)
10. T-shirts with vulgar, indecent, suggestive points, or anything which advertise or promote the use of drugs, alcohol, or tobacco.
11. Clogs (e.g., Crocs) may be worn if for medical reasons and during inclement weather (heavy rains and excessive heat).


### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/chtm-sharks-sinaliwan-ang-ritmo-tungong-panalo

# CHTM Sharks, sinaliwan ang ritmo tungong panalo
[Image: CHTM Sharks, sinaliwan ang ritmo tungong panalo]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, September 15, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE, OLONGAPO CITY—Nagliyab sa entablado ang College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Sharks sa Dancesports Competition—Latin Category nang ipamalas ang kanilang husay sa Cha-cha na sumubok sa indayog ng mga ritmo, Rumba na nagbigay-diin sa senswalidad, at Jive na nagpaigting ng sigla at enerhiya ng SIKLAB 2025.
Matapos ang tatlong antas ng laban, nangibabaw sina John Michael Apan at Cathlyn Heart Del Rosario ng CHTM Sharks. Ipinamalas nila ang matinding dedikasyon, tiyaga, at koordinasyon sa bawat hakbang at bawat himig ng musika. Tinanghal silang kampeon ng gabi sa kategoryang Latin.
Sinundan naman ito ng College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors na sumungkit sa ikalawang pwesto at College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Wizards sa ikatlong pwesto.
“Nagpapasalamat po ako sa Diyos sa guidance, also sa lakas. Unexpected po yung nangyari, aminado naman po kami na lahat ng department malalakas, also dahil nga po last year hindi namin nakuha ‘yong gold. Mas pinag-igihan na lang po namin yung training namin. Sobrang saya po namin na champion po kami,” ani ni John Michael, sa naramdamang galak matapos magwagi.
Dagdag pa ni Cathlyn, natural lang sa mag-partner ang mag-away. Hindi man daw nagkakasundo sa gustong steps; mga routines, mga gustong minsa’y hindi nauunawan. Ngunit, nagagawan naman ng paraan at naaayos sa usapan.
Hindi lamang sa tropeyo at ranggo umikot ang laban. Sa huli, ipinakita ng Latin Category ang sigasig at diwa ng komunidad ng Gordon College—kung saan ang bawat indak ay larawan ng determinasyon, pagkakaisa, at pagmamahal sa sining ng sayaw ng Gordon College Community. | via Lianne Malazarte/The FOREFRONT
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
Kuha ni Franz Feria
Layout ni John Patrick Mateo, Lance Isaac Leon
ERRATUM: The image used in the publication material before was another representative of CHTM. The champions of the Latin category are now shown in the edited material. We sincerely apologize for the confusion it may have caused.

# Strengthening Research Excellence: Instructors Oriented on IPR and Ethical Practices
[Image: Strengthening Research Excellence: Instructors Oriented on IPR and Ethical Practices]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, April 6, 2026**
The Research Instructors’ Orientation, Upskilling, and Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) Seminar Series, held on March 31, 2026, at the Cosme Y. Sering AVR, was successfully conducted with the active participation of faculty members and research instructors.
The seminar served as a valuable platform to reorient and enhance participants’ knowledge and competencies in key research processes, particularly in research ethics, research processes, intellectual property rights (IPR), and Research Integrity. Meaningful discussions and insights were shared, reinforcing the importance of ethical and responsible research practices within the academic community.
The activity was facilitated by Dr. Ediric D. Gadia (VP, RDCES) and Ms. Nicole Suero (Coordinator, PU and IPMU), whose expertise greatly contributed to the seminar’s success.
This initiative reflects the institution’s continued commitment to strengthening research capability and fostering a culture of excellence and integrity among its educators.

# Ballo and GCians Capture 1st and 2nd Runner-ups at Cultural Competition
[Image: Ballo and GCians Capture 1st and 2nd Runner-ups at Cultural Competition]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, December 23, 2025**
Gordon College’s (GC) Performing Arts Students Ensemble (PASE) Ballo and other students secured the 1st and 2nd runner-up titles in the Barangay Cultural Competition at Gordon Avenue, on December 20, at SM City Downtown.
Members of PASE Ballo and other GCians competed in their respective groups, representing Barangay Pag-asa and Barangay West Tapinac.
Barangay Pag-asa performed their Sagudsod de Mano, which led them to be placed as the first runner-up.
Meanwhile, Barangay West Tapinac earned their place as the second runner-up after grooving their way through their Inalimango performance.
Despite the wet roads due to the rain, the participants from the institution still showed their grace and effort in their renditions, embracing the Filipino culture.
The folk dance competition was a part of the Olongapo City Fiesta Celebration. | via Jasmine Zeah Llagas/The FOREFRONT

# Reading Literacy Training
[Image: Reading Literacy Training]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Saturday, June 10, 2023**
The GC CESU, Academic Affairs, and CEAS immersion instructors collaborated successfully to conduct a 3-day training for various CEAS academic programs. Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano played a pivotal role as the resource trainer, contributing to the training’s success. The training drew on the participation of a diverse group of student facilitators from ten programs within the College of Education, Arts, and Sciences. Specifically, 3rd-year students from BSEd (Mathematics, Science, English, Filipino, and Social Studies) and other programs like BPEd, BEEd, BCAEd, BECEd, and BAComm actively took part in the training. This training aligned with the goals of the MRP’s Reading Literacy Program for non-readers and out-of-school youth.
