The Journey of Becoming Professionals: The Experiences and Challenges of
BSEd Science Student Teachers through Teaching in Virtual Class
Hershelene Bueno, Abegail M. Castillon, Mark Arthur Escoto 1
Kristoffer Paul C. Obispo 2
1BSEd-Science Students, Gordon College
2Research Adviser: BSEd-Science, Gordon College
ABSTRACT
Practicum teaching is the most important aspect for becoming a professional
teacher since it provides student teachers with authentic and hands-on
experience and also allows the student teachers to put their idea into practice in
a real classroom setting. However, due to the outbreak of COVID-19, the classes
were moved virtually where student teachers also need to adjust to the new
normal setup. Thus, this study aimed to determine the experiences and
challenges of the BSEd–Science fourth year students of Gordon College during
their practicum teaching in a virtual classroom. Focused group interview method
was employed in this phenomenological qualitative research wherein the
researchers created a questionnaire that is composed of eleven (11) open-ended
questions that will identify the experiences and challenges of the fourth-year
education students majoring in Science in their practice teaching in a virtual
classroom. The findings of the study revealed the experiences of the participants
in terms of student’s performance and classroom management as well as their
challenges when it comes to student’s participation in class, with regards to their
availability of internet and devices, their environment and also with their
confidence in teaching. Data for this study were collected from 6 students of
BSEd–Science students enrolled in the school year of 2021-2022. The
researchers concluded that, teaching virtually is challenging due to
uncontrollable hindrances and disturbances that occurs during their teaching
internship, however, despite of the challenges occurred, the participants is able
to finish their practicum. Therefore, it is recommended to conduct studies that
associate additional factors and variables related to the study.
Keywords: experiences, challenges, student-teachers, practice teaching, virtual
classroom
INTRODUCTION
Practice teaching is the most important aspect for becoming a professional
teacher since it provides student teachers with authentic and hands-on experience
and also allows the student teachers to put their idea into practice in a real
classroom setting (Msangya et.al, 2016). Ulla (2016) also stated that practice
62

teaching prepares student-teachers to become qualified teachers in the future on
their own respective specialization. During this essential stage of a teaching career,
it will begin in developing the skills and adopt the values that shape one's approach
to teaching.
Practice teaching is integral to all teacher-education institution and also
teacher-education student or pre-service teachers as the conduct of this experience
allows pre-service teachers to apply theoretical knowledge learned during the
academic phase of their program (Roble & Bacabac, 2016). The practice teaching
is one of the requirements in finishing the degree in education (Ulla, 2016).
However, due to the outbreak of COVID-19, most of the schools in
Olongapo City were needed to close for the safety purposes of the students. With
that, the classes were moved virtually where student teachers also need to adjust
to the new normal setup. Hence, as an alternative to students’ teachers, they needed
to practice their teaching through online interaction with their students to continue
their practicum teaching, wherein the student teachers are expected to face various
challenges which might influence their teaching efficacy in practice.
This study was developed because of the interest of the researchers to
know the experiences and various challenges that faced by the BSEd Science
Student-teacher enrolled in Gordon College during their practice teaching through
virtual classes.
STATEMENT OF THE PROBLEM
This study generally aimed to determine the experiences and challenges of
the fourth year BSEd – Science of Gordon College in performing their practice
teaching in a virtual classroom. Specifically, this study sought to answer the
following:
1. What is the profile of the participants?
1.1 Age;
1.2 Sex;
1.3 Devices used in practice teaching.
2. What are the experiences of the participants in performing practice
teaching in virtual classroom in terms of:
2.1 Student’s performance;
2.2 Class Management?
3. What are the challenges of the participants in performing practice
teaching in virtual classroom in terms of:
3.1 Student’s participation;
3.2 Availability of Devices;
3.3 Internet availability;
3.4 Environment;
63

3.5 Confidence in teaching?
4. What are the implications of the study?
METHODOLOGY
Research Design
The study employed the phenomenological qualitative research as
Chambers (2013), Phenomenology is an approach to qualitative research that
focuses on the commonality of a lived experience within a particular group. The
fundamental goal of the approach is to arrive at a description of the nature of the
particular phenomenon. Additionally, Lambert and Lambert (2012) stated that the
goal of the qualitative descriptive studies is to summarize comprehensively the
specific events experienced by the participants which are the experiences and
challenges of the fourth-year education student when it comes to performing their
practice teaching in a virtual classroom.
Therefore, the main purpose of the study was to determine the experiences
and challenges of the fourth-year education student when it comes to performing
their teaching internship in a virtual classroom.
Respondents
The population is composed of fourteen (14) participants who are
currently studying at Gordon College as fourth-year education students majoring
in Science. The researchers used the criterion sampling as a sampling technique as
it is used to select sample based on a pre-established criterion by stating the
inclusion and exclusion criteria (Suri, 2011). As a result, the total number of
participants who participated in the study is six (6) participants wherein it is
gathered using the sampling technique mentioned.
Research Instrument
In conducting the study, the researchers created an interview-
questionnaire that is composed of eleven (11) open-ended questions that will
identify the experiences and challenges of the fourth-year education students
majoring in Science in their practice teaching in a virtual classroom.
The researchers contacted the participants to ask their consent and allow
the researchers to conduct a group discussion interview for the study. Afterward,
once the consent was secured, the researchers have provided a google meet link to
further start the interview.
Statistical Treatment of Data
64

Thematic analysis will be used in interpreting and analyzing the gathered
data. As indicated in the study conducted by Braun and Clarke (2006), this type of
data analysis involves the coding of qualitative data into clusters of comparable
articles, or conceptual categories, as well as the identification of consistent patterns
and links between themes in order to arrive at a theoretical explanation for the
phenomenon under investigation.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
I. Experience
Discussed in this part are the experiences of the practical teachers in terms
of student’s participation as well as the classroom management. The classroom
management discusses the preparation they do before they start a class as well as
solving several conflicts they encountered during virtual teaching. Furthermore,
the participants are asked a general question as to what their best experiences are
while in the field.
Q1. What are your best experiences while performing practice teaching in
virtual classroom?
Theme Sample Statement
Comfort of Home P1: “Having a controlled environment in the comfort of
my home. I can freely design and express my idea on
my own presentation such as power point presentation
that is related to my lesson”
Saves time and P2: “We are able to save time because as for me,
money travelling is more tiring than teaching. Cheap since we
don’t have to worry about”
Online Amenities P3: “Best experience is searching for simulations and
games online. There are variety of games that we can
use during virtual class. After using the games and
strategies that I’ve discovered online, my students
enjoyed it especially they are in 7th grade. It became
my trademark that my students wanted more of it.”
P4: “Usage or utilizing the media platforms that most
of the time we are not able to use during face to face
but now I was able to utilize more of it and incorporate
it in the class to have more fun and interactive and
visually and content wise platform.”
Connecting with the P4: “Having empathy towards them and to help them is
students the best experience I have. Although getting to know
65

the students in virtual class is hard, I still do my best to
know them for me to know what their need.”
P5: “I was able to connect myself with the students
especially as per Mr. P4, it was very hard to connect to
them since it’s virtual especially with only few
attendances due to most of them are in modular mode
of learning. The importance of being connected to your
student is that, you build the rapport and then you were
able to connect to them in a way that you know their
needs despite of the hardship that all of us are
experiencing.”
1.1. Does this help you to become a good teacher someday?
Participants: No, does not help me to become a good teacher someday
P1: “No because we need to be flexible in everything we do as a teacher. We will
have a hard time adjusting to face to face class if we’re used to virtual teaching.”
P2: “I think it’s not okay to teach virtually at home because It’s better to be at
school physically for us to experience how it feels to be a professional teacher.”
Participants: Yes! It helps me to become a good teacher someday
P3: “Definitely yes because we had the opportunity to have a new environment, a
new normal. We should be flexible and resourceful with what [materials] to use
and what strategy to utilize. It is a way to be an effective teacher someday.”
P4: “Yes. Having sympathy towards my student will help me to become a great
teacher someday.”
P5: “This is really a great help since even if it’s in virtual or in a face to face
classes, it is super important to connect to your students because it’s easier to
penetrate and implement whatever the lesson you are trying to deliver to them so
yes! It helps me to become a good teacher someday.”
P6: “Practically I would say yes, because in that case I think Im more of a
technologically inclined rather than those who have been into a face to face. So, I
can utilize media platforms very good. I’ve been using it for my practice teaching
that would be my edge I think. It will help me to become a good teacher because
I’m technologically inclined”
Due to subsequent lockdown, schools were forced to continue through
virtual platform (Winter et. al, 2021). Teachers are forced to cope up with the new
normal as these practical teachers has different perspective towards online teaching
modality.

to you especially when you’re

Technical preparation P1: “My preparation is learning and
mastering the lesson before I do the
68

lesson plan, and designing a power
point presentation. If I fail to do that, I
will not be able to fully deliver the
lesson and the content to the students
for that specific day and I will not be
able to attain the specific goal or
competencies.”
P2: “My coop teacher gives me a
physical copy of the module which I
use to learn the topic and while doing
so, my brain also thinks of the possible
activities I can use for modular
activities and online activities. After
that, I’ll prepare the Detailed Lesson
Plan and the Power Point
presentation.”
P3: “I and my coop teacher decided to
have a schedule to make our lesson
detailed plan and that’s every
Wednesdays.”
P4: “I double check the Presentation
and review my lessons as a mastery to
avoid being embarrassed in front of the
class.
P5: “I prepare my power point. I don’t
just save it in the app but I also
download its PDF and then I would
open it in chrome just in case the
power point app lags as in my case, if
I use software simultaneously, my
computer lags. And then my lesson
plan is always on just in case I get lost.
I also check if every link is also open.
One thing that is very important,
whenever you go through your topic
you put yourself into your student’s
shoes whether if they will be able to
understand your lesson. What are the
possible questions they may contract
69

into their minds so you can prepare an
answer in advance? My students in
ABM, although they’re few, they like to
scrutinize. They will squeeze you out to
the point where it’ll [topic] become a
debate. There are lots of “why” in
science so we have to prove it. One
thing is, in science, you cannot just
state any opinions or what you believe
in to. You must always have a
reference. If you don’t know the
answer, you must not hesitate to open
other tab to open a google.”
P6: “In terms of being a practice
teacher u need to have a detailed
lesson plan. Aside from that, an hour
before I start my class, I scan my
lesson plan because I should be
thoroughly prepared since if they pop
up some question and I couldn’t
answer, that is not good for a teacher.
I always make time to check my lesson
plan and also to not rely on a single
material but also browse other
websites, you tube and journals in
terms of the lesson I will be teaching
especially when it comes to
calculations and solving so I can
really teach them the derivation,
analyzation and solving problem”
Dealing with the often-overwhelming nature of the school placement
experience and the associated demands of classroom management can be a
significant challenge for many preservice teachers. Part of the challenge
experienced is the difficulty in applying theoretical knowledge related to behaviour
and classroom management during the practicum and dealing with the
overwhelming nature of the school placement experience where pupil behaviour is
just one of many challenges faced when beginning to teach (Wasmuth, 2016). A
second problem associated with this issue relates to the high-stakes nature of the
practicum experience, where assessment of the pre-service teacher’s competence
to teach overrides all other considerations (McGarr, 2021)
70

Q5. How are you able to solve conflicts inside the online class discussions? You
may cite examples.
Theme Sample Statement
Background noise P1: “The number one conflict for me is
the background noise, so basically I
resolve these types of conflicts quickly,
what I did was I turned off my
microphone and camera and for five
seconds I talked to my family member
or wherever that noise was coming
from.”
P2: “The conflict that I experienced
was the background noise, and I
resolved this type of conflict by quickly
turning off my microphone and
camera then I excused myself to the
class.”
P3: "The conflicts that I've
experienced were the background
noise, the problem with the internet
connectivity, and sudden blackout so
we need to have a quick reaction or
there should be a transition that will
happen there.”
P5: "The other conflicts that I
experienced was the technicalities
such as background noises, where I
had a time when I was teaching that
I'm sick, which my throat is so dry so I
keep on coughing during my class, so
I resolve this conflict by asking my
students politely to wait for me for
maybe 1-2 minutes because I really
need to drink water at that moment.”
Power interruption P2: "The first conflict I experienced
was the sudden power Interruption
which is I don't have an extra data
71

connection, so with this type of
conflict, you should be always
prepared when there are such
circumstances happens."
P3: "The conflicts that I've
experienced were the background
noise, the problem with the internet
connectivity, and sudden blackout so
we need to have a quick reaction or
there should be a transition that will
happen there.”
P5: "The conflict that I experienced is
the sudden power interruption, so in
this type of conflict you should have a
backup data connection to avoid
missing any other important lessons.”
Students’ sudden questions P2: “I have students with sudden
questions, whether the question is in
the lesson or not, you will be surprised
by their question. So what do I do
when I can't answer the question at the
moment, I'll make a script which is like
this "Okay, thank you for your
wonderful question, I will research on
that and I will get back to you on our
next meeting. "
Internet connectivity P3: "The conflicts that I've
experienced were the background
noise, the problem with the internet
connectivity, and sudden blackout so
we need to have a quick reaction or
there should be a transition that will
happen there.”
Groupings of students P3: “In groupings of students which is
sometimes, we have students who do
not participate in a group activity, so
therefore to resolve this kind of
conflict is at first you need to talk to
them and then if the student did not
really participate within their group
activity you should have another
72

option for them to have to do as an
individual activity. "
Students quizzes P4: “The conflict that I experienced is
about the students' quizzes, so to solve
this conflict, you should always need
to have a backup link or other methods
so that they can take that quiz on the
same day."
Students P6: "I experienced as if I had almost
no students, so in order to solve that
conflict I asked my students to open
their cameras through that I could see
them, and then they have no longer
excuses. And also when no one
answered, What I do is roll calling
which I'll call the names of the students
to know if they are really participating
in my class, or if they are being
passive-aggressive.”
Based on the data gathered from the respondents, most of them cited that
the conflict they usually encounter is background noise, and the other conflicts are
sudden power interruption, internet connectivity, students with shocking
questions, students' participation in a group activity, the quizzes of students, and
the students itself. Wherein these conflicts are immediately resolved by the
respondents with their own skills as well as knowledge in resolving conflicts that
arise during their practice teaching. Wherein, Conflict is something that is natural
and must be experienced by every individual, it has a positive impact as well as a
negative impact (Tuimur & Chemwei, 2015). For a conflict to have a positive
impact, individuals need to have conflict resolution skills such as knowing the
source of the conflict, who is in conflict, and the right strategies and tactics to
resolve the conflict (Gibbons, 2010).
II. Challenges
This part of the study identifies and discusses the challenges of student-
teachers in their teaching internship in a virtual set-up.
Q2. What are the challenges have you faced while in the field?
Theme Sample Statement

as the background noises and
settings.”

Knowing the students personally P4: “Another challenge that I have

students personally”
Time management P5: “And also time management for my
case because I am a working student”
Challenges such as uncontrolled environment, technical problems, student
monitoring, and time management are being faced by the science pre-service
teachers. The limited time to the online schedule for the delivery of lesson and the
technical and home environment also contribute to the difficulty in the online or
virtual classes.
Students Participation
Q6. During class discussions, what strategies do you use to make the students
actively participate? You may cite some examples.
Theme Sample Statement
I am the strategy as a teacher P1: "To be different, I am the strategy
as a teacher. I am the strategy where I
am the reason why they should
participate, I teach actively like a
clown or entertainer just to get the
attention of the whole class and this is
the strategy I did through my teaching
in virtual class.”
Finding the students’ like and P2: “I asked them to answer in the
dislike slam book so that I could find their
likes and dislikes because the most
effective strategy to make your
students actively participate is to site
clear examples related to their likes
and dislikes."
Localization P3: “When it comes to the strategy, we
have what is called localization where
the examples or the ones you should
relate during discussion should be
related to the interest of your students
when you are teaching.”
P5: "In teaching strategy, localization
is really very important in a way that
75

you bring it to your students into
comedy. And based on my experience,
the concept like this of localization
was what students want, wherein they
can relate to or find it funny so that
they can laugh at it. "
Roll call P4: "The strategies that I use for the
students to make them actively
participate in my class is I do a roll
call.”
Giving activities P4: “I give them an activity where
there is always needed a presentation
so the students can present their work,
wherein sometimes it is groupings or
individual."
Giving Prize P5: "The most effective strategy is to
give money to your students which is I
used this in one of my classes. But as a
teacher, you are not allowed to do that
which is bribing your students."
PowerPoint Presentation P6: " I notice that some of the students
are visual learners, so I put a lot of
animations in my PowerPoint
Presentation and then I also have
some video presentation because I
observe that some of them are
kinesthetic learners.”
Scaffolding Strategy P6: “I do a scaffolding strategy, so if
ever someone asks me and I want them
to answer that question for our topic,
I'll do a lot of follow-up questions so
they can get the best answer.”
In terms of strategies, the respondents have different ways of gaining the
attention of their students, wherein based on the data gathered the respondents cited
a strategy that they can use in their discussion to make their students actively
participate. And these strategies cited by the respondents are finding their student's
likes and dislike, localization which this strategy was answered by 2 of the 6
respondents, and the other strategies are they do roll call, giving activity, giving
money to the students, having an attractive PowerPoint presentation, scaffolding
strategy, and the teacher itself. These strategies cited by the respondents were
proven effective as they stated to the researchers because they already experienced
76

and apply them in their class virtually and according to Kyriacou (1998) once the
lesson has begun, the teacher’s main task is to maintain students’ attention, interest
and involvement in the activities which are at the same time educationally
effective. This is a real challenge which makes lesson management skills so
sophisticated.
Device and Internet Connectivity
Q7. How is the availability of devices and the internet affect your teaching
performance virtually?
Theme Sample Statement
Hinders good teaching P1: “having a stable internet
performance connection will help you to have a
good teaching performance and
deliver your lesson smoothly.”
P2: “So, when they [device] work well,
your performance in teaching will also
be good and smooth.”
P3: “It consumes too much time which
is really affecting my performance as
a teacher because my students are
waiting to see what the continuation of
our lesson will be.”
P4: “Since I teach virtually so the
laptop, cellphone, and stable internet
connection are the core of the whole
class so without them you can't teach.”
P5: “And also, the cellphone is very
helpful”
Does not hinder good teaching P6: “So in terms of that matter, I think
performance it's not a problem with my students
because they try to really give me their
attention and participate in my class
amidst the difficulties that we are both
facing.”
In a study by Fabito et al., (2020) the group revealed that one of the three
barriers and challenges that students encountered in online learning was a good
internet connection. A comparable idea from Casillano (2019) suggested that a
77

small percentage of students lack internet connectivity, which prevents them from
using the e-learning platform. Access to technology devices and internet
connectivity is one of the main obstacles to solving the learning problem. Asio
et.al (2021) supported the findings of this investigation and highlighted the
difficulties and requirements associated with students' internet connections.
Environment
Q11. Has your environment ever become a hindrance in your teaching? Based
on the hindrances that you listed, what do you think are the best way to deal with
those challenges?
Theme Sample Statement
Virtual Set-up P1: “Teaching in an online modality
setup is a both hindrance and an
advantage. Because I can’t be able to
see the behavior of my students.”
Students’ Participation P1: “I couldn’t really see if my
students are listening unless for those
who are reciting and participating
actively in class.”
Access on the Internet and Gadgets P1: “I have activities given using
social media platforms like wordwall.
In that platform, the whole activities
and its content can be seen if using
laptops or computers but for those who
are using mobile phones, they are
having hard time accessing it.”
P2: “Sudden internet connection and
even your laptop lagging.”
P3: “If your devices are defective or
cannot be properly used, you will have
a big problem to that. Also, those
unexpected disturbances like power
interruption and lagging of your
devices.”
Guidance P1: “My cooperation does not lack
with my department”
Mental Fortitude P2: “If you let yourself to not do
anything, and if you do not review your
78

lesson overnight, is the biggest
hindrance that you have.”
P3: “You do not have time to
breakdown as a student teacher.”
P5: “Sometimes you will experience
crying all of a sudden because of the
stress you are facing.”
Noises P2: “The uncontrollable environment,
like shouting and background noises
in your surroundings.”
P6: “Of course, the physical
environment like what they have
mentioned, those family members that
having small fights and those
unexpected animal sounds.”
Additional workloads P6: “Sometimes there are teachers that
who are asking you to make
PowerPoint presentation and the
like.”
The transition from face-to-face to online learning significantly affect the
student teachers as they have been forced to do their practicum, virtually, wherein
they can face different hindrances during their class discussion. Moreover, pre –
service teachers are concerned about the lack of comprehension or need further
information because they are not familiar with virtual teaching. Pre–service
teachers experienced several difficulties that they faced including the inability to
manage the classroom, inability to speak smoothly, and managing students’
behavior. (Safira, 2021)
Confidence in Teaching
Q8. How confident are you in teaching?
8.1 Are you confident that your students will be able to learn something from
you?
All Participants: “Yes, I'm confident”
P1: “Yes, I'm confident that they learned something from me as their teacher
because the feedback they give me is positive."
79

P2: "Yes, I'm very confident that my student is able to learn something from me
because the feedback is directly coming from my students which is positive that's
why I'm confident that they learned something from me as their teacher."
P3: "Yes, you should be confident because you will teach, therefore you will study
the topic to be taught carefully so with that you become confident and realized that
I can do it because I know what I am teaching.”
P4: "I know that my students have learned something from me so I am confident in
that part but speaking in front of the class is not because I am nervous and I am
scared, but I am fighting with that.”
P5: "Yes of course I’m confident, because what are we doing here if we can't teach
them, what's the use of your sleepless nights if your teaching isn't effective."
P6: "Actually yes I’m confident that my students will learn something from me
because aside from being a good speaker I think I execute the class well and teach
them the proper lesson in accordance with the most essential competencies”
Five out of 6 respondents answer that they are confident in teaching, while
the remaining 1 answered no because his confidence is low and he is afraid of his
students and he is nervous when teaching. However, all respondents answered
“Yes” when the question is about their confidence if their students learn something
from them. In the study of Lau (2021), teachers’ confidence improves the
performance of the teacher. Likewise, according to Beardsley et.al (2021),
teaching confidence and preparedness can improve teaching.
Q9. Have you felt anxious before class starts? Are you confident that your
students will be able to learn something from you?
Themes Sample Statement
Pressure P1: “I wasn’t feeling confident.
Suddenly, I feel pressured to the fact
that I will be the one teaching, will
execute the class.”
80

P2: “Because we have been shocked,
then we are being deployed
immediately.”
P4: “Of course, there is the
anxiousness occurring because of the
pressure, we are all very pressured.
Because we totally did not experienced
demonstration teaching.”
Low Self-esteem resulting P5: “My confidence in teaching is low
nervousness because I’m afraid of my students.”
P2: “It felt nervous, to the point that
even in introducing yourself, you are
nervous especially when you have
been assigned in Regional High
School where the students are
speaking in English. The moment you
hear your students talked, you will feel
that your self-esteem has been
decreased.”
Students’ participation P6: “My anxiety is when no one
attends in my class. It is very
disrespectful for me as a proactive
teacher.”
As pandemic started, teaching has move from the traditional learning into
an online or virtual one which makes the pre-service teachers faced some
unprecedented challenges that require them to adopt online teaching which also
makes them necessitated in imparting knowledge to students which was contingent
upon their confidence. Therefore, as a result, there are some categories that makes
the pre-service teacher anxious before the class starts such as their lack of teaching
experience and less of mastering the material before carrying out teaching activity.
Moreover, due to a lack of teaching experience, the pre-service teacher
encountered difficulties managing most especially the student’s behaviour
including their participation in the class. (Safira, 2021)
81

Q10. Do you feel excited when teaching? What factors excite/s you the most?
What are the least?
Themes Sample Statement
Mastery of concepts P1: “There are lessons that even I
myself, enjoy teaching it.”
P6: “I feel very excited whenever I am
teaching, the three years that you have
been studying the subject matter,
Finally, you will be able to share it to
your students.”
Feedback P2: “I already feel excited teaching in
the sense of when I heard the feedback
of my students, that made me feel
overwhelmed.”
P3: “Factors that excite me the most is
every after you teach, there is feedback
about how well you performed in
class, what improvements do you need,
and those strong points that you have.”

teaching, there are feedbacks coming
from my students.”

anymore because you are making LPs
for demos and yet it happened
simultaneously.”

82

Self-satisfaction P6: “The part that it mostly excites me
is that I can prove to myself that I am
doing properly, that I will be able to
become proud of myself.”
The understanding of one and perception of location, based on meanings
and relationships, is greatly influenced by emotion. Therefore, teaching practicum
could be seen as an emotional activity linked to the display of emotions in social
interactions. As a result, the excitement of pre-service teachers that they are feeling
is connected with their ability and their confidence regarding to teaching the
students. Furthermore, this excitement that they are feeling are a product of some
factors such as self-satisfaction as well as the feedbacks that they can get.
(Heryatun & Septiana, 2020)
Conclusion
After conducting the study, the researchers concluded the following:
1. During the entire experience of practice teaching of the participants,
anxiousness is inevitable and will be incorporated because of pressure.
Anxiety or nervousness is normal, but as they proceed in their
discussions, they can gradually overcome it.
2. At some point, doing practice teaching virtually is partly exciting and
not exciting due to different experiences in a virtual classroom. This
includes the student participation in the class.
3. Indeed, teaching virtually is also challenging because of the
uncontrollable hindrances and disturbances that may occur, such as
background noises, sudden power interruption of power and internet
connection, quality of the devices used, and teachers adding
workloads resulting to compression of time.
4. Time management is essential for a student teacher, as there are
overwhelming workloads that will occur and need to be done at the
same time.
5. Moreover, as a student teacher, mental health must not be overlooked
because there are times that they will feel exhausted and stressed. With
that, it is important to allot time to rest distress.
6. Despite multiple hindrances occurred, the participants thrive and has
survived the practicum.
83

Recommendations
This study revealed the experiences and challenges of BSEd – Science
fourth year students during their practice teaching in a virtual classroom. Based on
the summary of findings and conclusions, the following recommendations were
drawn:
1. Look at some additional factors and variables related to the experience
and challenges of student teachers who perform their practice teaching
in an online modality;
2. Use a large quantity of participants and use different program or
courses as their population to gain further accurate results as
experiences and challenges may vary;
3. Utilize the study in providing the needs of future teachers and students
in a virtual learning set-up;
4. Make further research regarding the experiences and challenges of
preservice teachers that performs their practicuum in a face-to-face
learning.

Physics Virtual Laboratories and Simulations: Challenges and Difficulties
Encountered by BSEd-Science Students of Gordon College
Melchor Gipal Jr. 1
Jenzel Troi Guevarra2
Hans Czaynrie Santos3
Kristoffer Paul C. Obispo4
123Researchers: BSEd-Science Students, Gordon College
4Research Adviser: BSEd-Science, Gordon College
Keywords Abstract
Experiences, The research aims to identify the challenges and difficulties
challenges, encountered by BSEd-Science Students of Gordon College in
student- having physics virtual laboratories and Simulations. The research
teachers, also seeks to determine whether the virtual lab allows students to
practice boost their self-paced learning. The online interview was used as
teaching, the primary data gathering method. The finding of the study which
virtual showed that it affects the learning of the students, most of the
classroom. respondents’ experience distraction mainly, social media, house
chores, personal dilemmas and technical aspects like unsupportive
software.
INTRODUCTION
Online class has been the new normal when it comes to our educational
setting during the pandemic and with the help of a multitude of platforms that can
be utilized to teach even with great distances, learning never stops but it comes
with difficulties that are unavoidable. The efforts of science teachers to manage
online learning have not optimized students' varied learning experiences because
science can't be learned by reading and discussion only, but also by hands-on
activities. (Wisanti et al. 2021)
According to Sypsas and Kalles (2018) Laboratory classes are essential in
Biology, Biotechnology and Chemistry, as they help learners acquire skills and
understand physical phenomena in depth. To run hands-on laboratories one needs
space, equipment and staff, so virtual laboratories can help reduce the total cost
and still complement physical presence. During this pandemic, laboratory
experiment and exercises seems harder to do due to different circumstances we are
facing within each other’s home since privileges is not similar to every student,
one might find this difficult to execute, it might be too expensive to others or it
lacks the essential skill acquisition that only hands-on experience can provide.
Many science researchers attempt to answer such a question through
87

studies that focus on the effects of computer simulations on students’
understanding of science concepts as well as on motivation. These researchers
studied the effectiveness of computer simulations from different points of view.
Some researchers have studied the efficiency of computer simulations compared
to the traditional method (Chen & Howard, 2010).
It was concluded that the developed virtual chemistry laboratory software
is at least as effective as the real laboratory, both in terms of student achievement
in the unit and students’ ability to recognize laboratory equipment, Tatli and Ayas
(2013). With this in hand, students are able to grasp concepts in chemistry without
the aid of real life or hands-on experiments due to the effectiveness of laboratory
simulations and software.
Online education has demonstrated comparable learning gains when
analyzed to those of the traditional classroom, but research is mixed when
reviewing students’ ability to manipulate tangible laboratory equipment after
participating in online experimentation. The question remains, are students who
are exclusively enrolled in online science courses equipped with the cognitive
ability to operate laboratory equipment within a physical laboratory? When
considering the optimal learning environment for science majors, educators have
discovered the blended classroom may provide the perfect opportunity to combine
the benefits of face-to-face instruction and feedback with the reinforcement of
scientific theory through technology integration. (Rivera, 2016)
Over the last two decades a great deal of educational research has been
directed towards the exploration of students' ideas and difficulties on physical
concepts and processes, Driver et al. 1985; Duit, et al. 1991). Physics simulations
on the other hand do lack the hands-on experience unlike with the chemistry
simulations which don't fully require the hands-on part to fully grasp the concepts
taught within laboratories.
STATEMENT OF THE PROBLEM
This study is conducted to identify the major difficulties and the factors
that result in the major difficulties in Physics Virtual Laboratories and Simulations
among 3rd year students of BSEd-Science in Gordon College.
This study seeks to answer the following questions:
1. Respondent profile is described by the following
1.1. Device/s used for online classes
1.2. Device/s owned for online classes
1.3. Wi-Fi or Data user
1.4. Internet Speed/Latency (megabits per second) (applicable for WIFI
users)
88

1.5. Signal Strength (1-4 bars)
1.6. Employment Status
1.7. Family Income (Monthly)
2. Identification of difficulties and relating them to the factors or the student’s
profile:
2.1. What are the major difficulties experienced by the majority?
2.2. What are the associating factors with the major difficulty?
2.3. How significant the difficulty is to the associating factor/s?
3. What are the student’s other queries aside from those mentioned in the
student’s profile?
METHODOLOGY
This chapter describes the study's methodologies and processes, which
were utilized by the researcher to gather, analyze, and interpret the research's data
needs. This chapter also covers the study design, respondents, research
instruments, instrument construction, data collection technique, and statistical data
analysis
Research Design
Phenomenological qualitative research design was the chosen design by
the researchers. Researchers will gather qualitative data in order to complete and
refine them. “A set of guidelines and instructions to be followed in addressing the
research problem” (Mouton, 1996:107).
For the qualitative part of the study, semi-structure virtual research is
carried out in order to reveal the opinions of BSEd Science 3rd year students of
Gordon College about utilizing virtual laboratory applications.
The characteristic feature of this design is that the study is done with one
survey questionnaire for measuring the instrument in collecting data and to reveal
the difficulties encountered by Based Science 3rd year students of the said school
in utilizing virtual laboratory application.
Respondents
The respondents of this study are the BSEd Science 3rd year students of
Gordon College, Olongapo City who were enrolled in the year 2021-2022. Among
the participants of this study, 15 were males and 6 were females.
The researcher used non-probability sampling because it was very
acceptable for this study. Non-probability sampling is a sampling technique where
the samples are gathered in a process that does not give all the individuals in the
population equal chances of being selected. There is also a risk that it may produce
89

less accurate and misleading results. But based on the capability, experience,
budget of the researcher and the time span that the research will take place, it is
more convenient and practical like most of the samples in nursing research (Polit
& Beck, 2006).
Research Instrument
The researchers will utilize online interviews by using a questionnaire
through Google Meet, Messenger Video Call, or zoom. The information that will
be collected in the survey made the study strengthen its standard. The online
interviews were conducted on current 3rd-year BSEd-Science students at Gordon
College.
The questionnaire is designed to acquire information about the experience
of the respondents on utilizing virtual laboratories in the classroom. The researcher
created the questionnaire after reviewing reference materials and thinking about
how to use the virtual laboratory in class.
Statistical Treatment of Data
The third-year BSEd-Science students' responses to the interview
questionnaire will be thematically analyzed to meet the study's data criteria. The
number of responders who answered similarly in a certain item or question will
influence the frequency of each response. The researchers gradually formed a
result based on the answers provided by the respondents.
Thematic analysis is the often-used method of qualitative research,
providing concise description and interpretation in terms of themes and patterns
from a data set. The application of thematic analysis requires trained expertise and
should not be used in a prescriptive, linear, and inflexible manner while analyzing
data (Majumdar, 2018).
Owing to its flexibility, thematic analysis, however, can never be criticized
being devoid of scientific temperament. The method of thematic analysis consist
of very specific and clear guidelines for its conduction, and these procedural
guidelines no doubt give the method a scientific vigor (Majumdar 2018), (Braun
and Clarke 2006), while propounding the six-step process of analysis in thematic
technique, have not only focused on the procedural concepts like “what”, “why”,
“when” and “how” of the method concerned but have also specified that the analyst
should have clarity and immense technical expertise to carry out the analysis
through thematic method. This touch of procedural simplicity mingled with
complex technical background gives thematic analysis a detailed yet rich flavor in
comparison to other qualitative analysis methods, and henceforth makes it the most
popular and widely used method for qualitative data analysis.
90

This chapter contains the results, analysis, and interpretation of the
problem under investigation.

As shown in table 8, the respondents experienced many difficulties and it
ranges from environmental, technical, communication up to psychological barriers
that hinders the learning or affects by different means. It gives a broad spectrum
of difficulties hence creating the said analysis funnels the respondents and defining
most in more definitive stature.
One of the prominent problems within the execution of physics
simulations are the external factors like environmental barriers, the noises created
by the environment and responsibilities of the students hinders the respondents in
performing the said simulations since home setting mixes up the respondents’
tasks. Social media also has its fair share of dilemma when it comes to determining
the difficulties encountered by the respondent, since the medium the respondents
are using for the simulations are the same medium used for entertainment purposes
thus home setting (environment) gives the majority or most of the challenges to
the respondents. The general studies and the research of the pedagogical
experiment confirm that the best results in learning content can be achieved by
using a mixed teaching method, i.e., virtual lab physics should only be used
together with the traditional method, choosing methodically which of the
laboratory works will be done virtually in the traditional way. As the results show
with the help of the virtual laboratory it is possible to strengthen students' ability
to read and interpret graphic information, but to learn to work with text and
understand the context, traditional methods are better. Cooperation and
communication skills are strengthened by traditional works when respondents are
forced to cooperate and help each other, which is necessary during traditional
laboratory work. (Juskaite 2019).
Environment
Research has found that learning environments play a crucial role in
student success. Several factors can affect learning ability, including seating, light,
noise, and even color. Students who study in a positive learning environment have
been shown to be more motivated, engaged, and have a higher overall learning
95

ability. On the other hand, students learning in poor environments – those that are
uncomfortable, loud, or full of distractions – will find it far more difficult to absorb
information and stay engaged. With this in mind, let’s look at how your
surroundings affect the way you study, and consider some of the best ways to
create your ideal learning environment. (Hendrix,2019)
More than one in three students could not study at home as they were
engaged in household chores during the pandemic, a regional study has found. Of
the 396 who participated in the UNICEF’s U-Report South Asia poll, the biggest
concern for more than 100 Bhutanese youth was about not having time to study at
home due to household work during the Covid-19 pandemic. The findings also saw
that almost an equal number of youths feel they were given too much schoolwork.
No or limited access to online learning, worry about the exam, and difficult to
study in the home environment were their other major concerns. (Rinzin,2020)
Technical Difficulties
Pre-existing disparities among students in terms of socio-economic status
and territorial origin have worsened. For instance, the National Conference of State
Legislatures (NCSL) has reported that 20% of college students in the US have
issues accessing effective technology such as working laptops and reliable
highspeed connection to pursue their higher education online. In particular,
students living in remote or rural areas have struggled to benefit from the
advantages of digital education. Cases have been reported of students unable to
follow their courses because of their insufficient or unstable internet connection,
inadequate resources such as laptops that bug all the time, or their impossibility to
find a calm environment to study. (Girard et al, 2021)
Communication
Students still prefer classroom classes over online classes due to many
problems
they face when taking online classes, such as lack of motivation, understanding of
the material, decrease in communication levels between the students and their
instructors and their feeling of isolation caused by online classes. Research
limitations/implications This research studied the impact from students'
perspective only as the sample was selected only from students. Originality/value
This research reached the students’ point of view in a broader way which will help
understanding the issues and provide effective solutions. This research suggested
that instructors must communicate with their students and vice versa in more
informal channels (instant messages online chat groups, audio calls, private video
calls …) in parallel with the formal channels (online platforms, email …). Finally,
instructors should encourage students to participate and study more by providing
different kind of incentives. (Alawamleh et al, 2020).
96

Physiological
The purpose of this study was to identify the impact of student psychology
on online learning during the COVID-19 pandemic. The research method uses a
qualitative research type of phenomenology. The research subjects were 30
students of Mulawarman University who were interviewed via telephone. The
research findings show that (1) students have started to get bored with online
learning after the first two weeks of learning from home, (2) considerable anxiety
on research subjects whose parents have low income, because they have to buy
quotas to be able to participate in online learning, (3) mood or mood changes occur
due to too many assignments and are considered ineffective by students.
Suggestions and recommendations from this research are the need for severe
efforts in assisting the psychological well-being of students through the
involvement of counselors and psychologists. (Irawan et al, 2020)
Personal dilemmas also have their contribution into respondents’
difficulties. This affected them in terms of coping abilities, attention span, and the
over-all feel of the online modality made them feel lazier than before during the
face-to-face era.

environment

As shown in the tables 9 and 10, the respondents responded positive and
negative views within the simulations and they still have a fair share of good news
and bad news. According to the interview, the simulations did help them in most
cases and still provided meaningful learning in the right setting but some new
concerns arose from the said survey. The respondents do worry about the actual
performing of the experiment or simulations since it has a great impact when taught
in person and with hands-on experience that the simulations can never give to the
respondents. Insecurities within the said learning experience is always present
since the respondents do know that they can perform better in the faceto-face class
and the entire experience within the simulations are compromised.

As shown in the table 11, recommendations are gathered within the heart’s
content of the respondents to expound their total immersion within the online
modality of the virtual laboratory experience. These recommendations are set to
be useful in the creation of the action plan.
Conclusion
The findings were to funnel down to conclude that:
 In accordance to the profile:
 Only one respondent is working and the rest are non-working.
 Family income is enough to support data and internet
connection.
 Devices required for physics simulations are present for
everyone
 Most of the respondents’ experience distraction mainly, social media,
house chores, personal dilemmas and technical aspects like unsupportive
software.
 As for their recommendations:
 Professors’ involvement in most simulations are highly
demanded or suggested
 Collaboration or peer groups were also suggested
 Simulations must be user friendly since most of the simulations
are too outdated and has limited instructions.
Recommendations
Based on the study and analysis of the answers of survey questionnaires
and feedbacks from the respondents, this study makes the following
recommendations:
 Conduct a study that asks similar queries but in a different field
102

like biology or chemistry.
 Increase the number of respondents.
 Do more readings and study further topics regarding the current
topic.

Certo Eruditio The Official Research Journal of

ABOUT THE JOURNAL
The Gordon College- College of Education, Arts, and Sciences is
committed to fostering a culture of research excellence as one of the trifocal
function of a higher education institution. The college annually published
the Certo Eruditio to recognize the significance of the scholarly inquiry in
advancing knowledge and addressing challenges in the community and
society as a whole.
Certo Eruditio features a diverse range of research articles spanning
various themes and areas within the field of institutional research. These
themes are the research agenda of Gordon College, fostering a
multidisciplinary approach to addressing pressing issues in education, arts,
and sciences.
The following themes are drawn from Gordon College's research agenda,
with particular focus directed to the means by which it corresponds to the
SDGs (Sustainable Development Goals) in the areas of education, the arts,
and the sciences:
1. Policy-Oriented Research Studies: This research area focuses
on the different dimensions of policy review, formulation,
implementation, monitoring and evaluation. It main purpose is to
look into the efficiency and effectiveness of the different policies
relative to the governance and management of Gordon College as
institution of higher learning, focus will be given to education, arts,
and sciences. It ensures alignment with SDG 4 (Quality Education)
by assessing policies that enhance educational quality and SDG 16
(Peace, Justice, and Strong Institutions) by promoting effective
institutional governance.
2. Studies on Academic/Curricular Programs: This research area
focuses on analysis and assessment of the curricular programs
currently implemented in the different academic fields /disciplines.
It intends to determine how the different academic programs of
College of Education, Arts, and Sciences could be improved,
revised or development of a curriculum that is relevant to needs of
the society. It supports SDG 4 (Quality Education) by enhancing the
relevance and inclusivity of educational programs to meet societal
needs.
3. Instructional Production and Development Studies: Dealing
with the creation and development of instructional materials, this
topic covers the teaching and learning process in education, arts
ii

Certo Eruditio The Official Research Journal of
College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS)
Volume 6 2023 ISSN: 2546-1710
and sciences. It supports SDG 4 (Quality Education) by improving
the availability and effectiveness of educational resources.
4. Gender-Responsive Studies: This discipline promotes inclusion
and diversity by promoting gender equality in the fields of education,
arts and science. It aligns with SDG 5 (Gender Equality) by creating
the same inclusive educational environment.
5. Community-Responsive and Extension-Oriented Studies: By
applying academic, artistic, and scientific knowledge to meet the
needs of the community, the discipline contributes to community
development and sustainability. It aligns with SDGs including SDG
11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities) and SDG 17
(Partnerships for the Goals) through partnerships to achieve
sustainable development.
6. Mode/Paradigm Building Studies: This research area focuses on
the development of models and paradigm which are deemed
appropriate in the new education landscape/setting. It aligns with
SDG 4 (Quality Education) to ensure inclusive and quality education
and promoting lifelong learning by developing models and
paradigms in adapting to new landscape and setting in the field of
education and communication.
7. Networks and Linkages Studies: This research theme focuses on
establishing a cooperative and a collaborative linkage with local,
national and international by groups or entities. This theme ensures
and enhances interdisciplinary and multidisciplinary collaboration
and knowledge exchange in education, arts, and sciences. It aligns
with SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals) by promoting
collaboration for sustainable development.
8. Creative Works: This research theme focuses on but not limited to
visual, textual, designs, film, and other related creative works which
is intended for innovative, scientific or technical form of research.
This could align to SDGs 4 (Quality Education), 9 (Industry,
Innovation, and Infastructure), 11 (Sustainable Cities and
Communities), 13 (Climate Action), and 17 (Partnerships for the
Goals). Here are some concept to relate SDG to Creative Works”
a) SDG 4 (Quality Education): Creative Works can contribute
in the creation of innovative and effective educational
approaches to enhance learning and promote creativity and
critical thinking skill for all learners.
b) SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure): It can
drive innovation in various industries to foster technological
advancement and sustainable development.
iii

Certo Eruditio The Official Research Journal of
College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS)
Volume 6 2023 ISSN: 2546-1710
c) SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities): It can
play a critical role in molding or shaping cultural identity and
sustainable development by contributing to vibrant and
inclusive communities.
d) SDG 13 (Climate Action): Creative works can raise
awareness regarding issues on our environment to inspire
action leading to the mitigation of climate change and its
effect through artistic expression and storytelling and the
likes.
e) SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals): Collaboration and
partnership are integral in the creative industries as well as
in the field of education, to facilitate exchange of knowledge,
best practices, and innovation to achieve the sustainable
development goals.

MESSAGE FROM THE OFFICE OF THE VICE PRESIDENT FOR
ACADEMIC AFFAIRS AND THE OIC-DEAN OF COLLEGE OF
EDUCATION, ARTS, AND SCIENCES (CEAS)
It is with great pleasure and admiration that I extend my heartfelt
congratulations to the entire team behind the publication of Certo Eruditio,
the esteemed research journal of the College of Education, Arts, and
Sciences. Your tireless efforts and unwavering commitment to scholarly
excellence have culminated in this remarkable achievement, marking a
significant milestone in the academic landscape.
Scholarly journals such as Certo Eruditio serve as a guiding light in
providing intellectual enlightenment to a world that is constantly seeking
information and creativity. These journals encourage the sharing of
innovative ideas and concepts, thereby enhancing the academic
community and inspiring future generations to strive for excellence in their
academic pursuits. By providing a platform for academics, researchers,
and scholars to communicate their ideas and discoveries, Certo Eruditio is
contributing significantly to the field of scholarly research.
Certo Eruditio's publication is a testament to the commitment and
knowledge of the editorial board, reviewers, writers, and contributors who
have assiduously strived to maintain the highest levels of scholarly integrity
and rigor. The intellectual landscape has been profoundly impacted by your
combined efforts, which have surely advanced knowledge across multiple
fields.
In honor of this important milestone, let us once again pledge to
promote academic excellence, encourage interdisciplinary discourse, and
cultivate a culture of intellectual inquiry and discovery. I hope that Certo
Eruditio will continue to be a driving force behind innovation and scholarly
discourse, enabling academics to push the envelope of knowledge and
significantly advance their disciplines.
Again, I would like to express my sincere congratulations to the
entire Certo Eruditio team. I appreciate your commitment, enthusiasm, and
intellectual contributions, and I am confident that the journal will flourish
and inspire upcoming generations of scholars for many years to come.
DARWIN P. PAGUIO, LPT, PhD

OIC-DEAN, CEAS

Effectiveness of English-Filipino as a Medium of Instruction In
Understanding Mathematics on Grade 7 Students
Josue M. Beloria Jr. 1, Queency Yvonne L. Quiniola 2,
John Paul C. Ramasamy 3, Darwin P. Paguio 4
1, 2, 3 BSED-Mathematics Students, College of Education, Arts, and Sciences
4 Faculty, Research Adviser, College of Education, Arts, and Sciences
Keywords Abstract
Experiment, The purpose of this study is to conduct an
English-Filipino experiment where the researchers examined the
Instruction, effectiveness of English-Filipino as a medium of instruction
Mathematics on Grade 7 students’ performance towards Mathematics. It
Performance, aims to assess the profile of the students in terms of the
Quasi- results from a control group, determine the performance of
Experimental the Grade 7 students exposed to English based instruction,
Research, compare students’ performance in mathematics using
Pretest, Posttest, English-Filipino based instruction using the results for the
Reliability, students mean. A quasi-experimental research design was
Paired T-Test, employed, involving two groups of Grade 7 students from
Effectiveness, Olongapo City National High School (OCNHS) during the
Classroom school year 2022-2023 for the evaluation. A total of 48
Interactions, Grade 7 students, divided into two groups, participated in
Language the experiment to deduce from their performance. Two
Integration, groups, namely the experimental and control groups,
Academic underwent pretests and posttests based on their respective
Improvement groupings. The reliability of the instrument was assessed
through descriptive statistics, and paired t-tests for paired
samples were employed to analyze the data. The findings
show that the grade 7 students’ have a favor using English-
Filipino based instruction in Mathematics.
INTRODUCTION
Education is crucial for everyone, and language plays a pivotal role in the
educational process. However, students who commence their schooling in a
language they are not proficient in encounter significant hurdles. This is because
they struggle to comprehend the language used in the classroom, which impedes
their learning progress. Filipino students often grapple with this issue, given that
English serves as the predominant language of instruction in schools.
Consequently, students may experience feelings of frustration and
discouragement, making it challenging for them to excel academically. Tagalog,
one of the primary languages spoken in the Philippines, serves as the first
language for many inhabitants of the country.
Tagalog region of northern Luzon. English was introduced into the
Philippine educational system during the American occupation in the early 1900s,
1

Certo Eruditio The Official Research Journal of
College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS)
Volume 6 2023 ISSN: 2546-1710
and it has remained an important part of the educational system ever since.
Learning English is essential for students in the Philippines, as it is the language
of business, government, and academia. Santos et al. (2022) emphasizes the
importance of learning English for effective communication. Students who are
proficient in English are better able to understand their teachers and classmates,
and they are also more likely to find employment in jobs that require English
language skills. However, there has been a decline in English proficiency in the
Philippines in recent years. The Philippines is no longer ranked as the top English-
speaking nation in the world.
Currently, the Philippine Constitution names English as one of its official
languages. It serves as the main teaching method in schools (Cabigon, 2015). The
learning of mathematics by students for whom English is a second language
creates significant challenges when English is the language of instruction as it
affects how well children understand and apply mathematical concepts, language
proficiency is essential for children's mathematical knowledge. Poor language
proficiency is associated with poor mathematics achievement because language
proficiency can affect mathematics performance (Abedi, 2004; Martiniello, 2008).
Students may learn less effectively when math is taught in a language that they
are not fluent in. To address this challenge, Filipino math teachers often use a
communication strategy that combines two languages.
According to Siyang (2018), using a student's first language as the primary
medium for teaching mathematics holds significant importance for student
achievement in the Philippines. They cite a study conducted by the Department of
Education (DepEd), which found that students who were taught mathematics in
their mother tongue outperformed those who received instruction in English. This
implies that utilizing the first language as the medium of instruction can yield
favorable outcomes for students' mathematical learning in the Philippines.
Employing the first language for math instruction contributes to enhanced
academic performance. This improvement in math achievement may also
positively affect other subjects, as students cultivate stronger problem-solving and
critical thinking abilities. When students are taught in a language, it reduces
language-related barriers making it easier for them to understand and engage with
the subject. In a language that the learners are not familiar with, mathematical skills
are arguably difficult to learn and master. The students struggle to understand and
frequently have difficulties to learn mathematical concepts and skills (Siyang,
2018). This has led students to have problems in learning mathematics as it
involves complex concepts and requires a high level of comprehension.
Karikari et al. (n.d.) discovered that employing students' first language as
the instructional medium for upper primary math classes in rural Ghana can
markedly enhance math learning outcomes. This approaches teachers to integrate
students' mother tongue into math instruction, which can boost math performance
and help students develop a stronger foundation in the subject. By making math
more accessible and relatable to their students, teachers can promote an
understanding of mathematical concepts and a love for the subject. In the
Philippines, we can strongly relate to this concept, as not everyone here finds it
easy to understand English. By incorporating students' first languages into math
2

Certo Eruditio The Official Research Journal of
College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS)
Volume 6 2023 ISSN: 2546-1710
instruction, we can make the subject more accessible and relevant to a wider range
of learners.
It is highly significant for math teachers to employ straightforward language
to establish connections between mathematical concepts and the tasks at hand.
This practice not only enhances students' understanding but also lays a strong
groundwork for their mathematical education (Yeo et al, 2019). In mathematics, it
has its own special language, and students need to understand these words. If
they can't understand these words, they'll have trouble with math concepts
therefore, math teachers should use simple words to help students connect
mathematics ideas with what they need to do. Pillos et al. (2020) emphasized that
utilizing the first language as the medium of instruction for teaching and
understanding mathematical problem-solving enhances learners' understanding of
concepts and problem-solving strategies. Combining the use of the mother tongue
with a global language like English is an optimal strategy for preparing students for
broader educational and career opportunities. It provides students with a solid
grounding in their first language, which is essential for their cultural identity and
sense of self-worth.
Bagri (2022) argues that teaching mathematics in Mandarin Chinese can
help students better understand and grasp mathematical concepts because they
are using a language, they are most comfortable with and familiar with. This is
because students are more likely to understand the vocabulary and grammar used
in math instruction when it is in their mother tongue, and they are also more likely
to be engaged in their learning and develop a positive attitude. The strong
performance of Chinese students in mathematics is often attributed to the use of
their own language in teaching the subject.
According to Civan (2016), learning in a language that's not primary can
be tough. But others argue that English materials are often better. It may be
possible to use a combination of the first language and a non-first language for
instruction. This approach can allow students to benefit from the strengths of both
languages. Teaching students in a non-first language is to use a variety of methods
and to tailor the instruction to the needs of the individual student. Teachers should
recognize and be considerate of the challenges students face in acquiring
mathematical language skills. They should also demonstrate creativity and
perseverance in devising strategies to assist students in their learning process.
Additionally, having a solid understanding of common language difficulties
can simplify the planning of effective teaching and learning experiences (Jourdain,
2016). Teachers can employ a variety of strategies, resources, and a supportive
classroom environment to help students overcome these challenges and excel in
mathematics.
Statement of the Problem
This study seeks to assess grade 7 students’ understanding of Mathematics using
the English-Filipino as a supplementary medium of instruction in Olongapo City
3

Certo Eruditio The Official Research Journal of
College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS)
Volume 6 2023 ISSN: 2546-1710
National High School.
Specifically, it aims to find answers to the following questions:
1. How may pretest and posttest results of the learners be described in terms
of:
1.1. control group; and
1.2. experimental group?
2. Is there a significant difference between the pretest and posttest of grade
7 students in terms of instruction-based learning when presented with
English-Filipino based instruction compared to English based instruction?
3. Is there a significant difference of the students mean scores of the
students using the results of the:
3.1. pretest; and
3.2. posttest in the experimental and control group?
METHODOLOGY
Research Design
A quasi-experimental research design was employed, involving two
groups of Grade 7 students from Olongapo City National High School (OCNHS)
during the academic year 2022-2023, to assess the effectiveness of using English-
Filipino as a supplementary medium of instruction on students' Mathematics
performance. A total of 48 Grade 7 students, divided into two groups, participated
in the experiment to ascertain the impact of the mother tongue as a supplementary
medium of instruction on their performance. Pretests and posttests were
administered to gauge any significant differences in students' Mathematics
performance.
Respondents
To gather data from the population, the researchers used one-stage
cluster population sampling. Cluster sampling, as described by Julia Simkus
(2023) is a probability sampling method where researchers can take a
representative sample from a population that has been split up into groups. In one-
stage sampling, the researchers aim to directly select a sample where every
element within the selected cluster will become a part of their sample group. This
sampling method is feasible to select a sample since the target population is small
and homogeneous. Overall, this study population consisted of students enrolled in
two specific sections within the 7th grade at Olongapo City National High School
during the Academic Year 2022-2023. The study involved a total of 44 participating
students, with 22 students allocated to Section 2 and another 22 to Section 5,
representing the entire population under investigation.
Research Instrument
Pre-test and post-test were used as standardized questionnaires in this
research. The experimental and control groups utilized identical and parallel
lessons; however, the language of instruction utilized varied. The lesson plan was
based on the Teacher's Guide published by the Department of Education. The
questionnaires were based on the 3rd quarter, grade 7 lessons following the
content of each module the learners will tackle in the 4 sessions the researchers
observed. The experimental group underwent instruction in Mathematics using
4

Certo Eruditio The Official Research Journal of
College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS)
Volume 6 2023 ISSN: 2546-1710
English-Filipino-based instruction, while the control group received instruction
solely in English. The lesson plans for the control group were prepared in English,
while those for the experimental group were crafted in English-Filipino, with
translations validated by two mathematics teachers to ensure accuracy and
correctness.
Statistical Treatment of Data
The data from this study were analyzed using a variety of statistical
techniques. The comparison of pre-test and post-test scores from both sections,
as well as the math test scores of Grade 7 students exposed to English-Filipino-
based and English instruction, was conducted using descriptive statistics. (1)
Frequency distribution was utilized to describe the pre-test and post-test scores of
both the control and experimental groups. (2) The significance of the difference
between the pre-test and post-test results was evaluated using a paired t-test. (3)
A t-test was employed to determine the significant difference in the effectiveness
of English-Filipino as a supplementary medium of instruction, comparing the pre-
test and post-test scores between the control and experimental groups.
Results and Discussion
This part presents the scores of the respondents regarding their pre-test
and posttest scores. The scores consist of their performance which ranges from
poor, fair, good, and excellent.

Table 1 illustrates the pretest and posttest results of students belonging to
the control group. The distribution of pretest and post-test scores within the control
group indicates discernible trends. Initially, among the 24 student respondents, the
pretest results indicate a diverse distribution of scores. Specifically, 4 students
(16.7%) obtained poor scores, while 9 students (37.5%) achieved fair scores.
Additionally, 5 students (20.8%) attained good scores, and 6 students (25%)
received excellent scores. On average, the pretest score for the control group was
8.71. These percentages signify the classification of students based on their
pretest performance.
In contrast, post-test scores exhibited a shift, demonstrating an overall
improvement. None of the students scored below 5, indicating progress across the
board. Specifically, 7 students (29.2%) achieved fair scores, while a significant
5

Certo Eruditio The Official Research Journal of
College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS)
Volume 6 2023 ISSN: 2546-1710
proportion, 12 students (50%), obtained good scores. Additionally, 5 students
(20.8%) earned excellent scores. The average post-test score for the control group
was notably higher at 9.92.
Pre-tests serve as crucial tools for activating students' prior knowledge and
establishing a foundation for the introduction of new concepts (Brown & Jones,
2018). Furthermore, they have the potential to enhance student motivation by
emphasizing the importance of forthcoming material and setting targets for
improvement (Thomas, 2017). Post-tests, on the other hand, offer valuable
feedback to both students and educators. They provide insights that inform future
instructional decisions and facilitate the monitoring and adjustment of teaching
strategies (Wilson & Johnson, 2022).

Table 2 displays the pretest and posttest scores of the experimental group.
The frequency distribution of pretest and posttest scores in the experimental group
offers valuable insights into the efficacy of instructional approaches. Initially, the
pretest results revealed that none of the 24 participating students scored below 5.
Specifically, approximately 13 students (54.1%) were classified as having a fair
score, while 9 students (37.5%) achieved a good score. Moreover, 2 students
(8.4%) were classified as having an excellent score. The average pretest score for
the experimental group was 8.79.
Upon analyzing the posttest scores, it was observed that none of the
students attained poor or fair scores. A majority of the students, specifically 70.8%
or 17 students, received a good score. Additionally, 29.2% or 7 students were
classified as having an excellent score. The mean posttest score for the
experimental group showed a significant increase to 10.88.
Furthermore, it was noted that students who received mathematics
instruction in their first language outperformed those who learned exclusively in
English. This indicates that the experimental group, which received instruction in
their first language, performed better overall compared to the control group, which
received instruction in English alone. Additionally, students taught in their first
language were more likely to achieve high scores on the posttest. In contrast to
the control group, the experimental group showed a greater tendency to achieve
good or excellent scores on the posttest (Lee et al., 2013).

conclude that there is a difference in scores between the pretest and posttest for
the control group. Verschik (2014) and Lee (2017) proposed that incorporating
code-switching in math classes may assist in bridging the proficiency gap between
the first and second language, thus enhancing understanding of complex
mathematical concepts for bilingual students.

-4.059, p = .000] at the 5% significance level. This suggests that there is sufficient
evidence to support the effectiveness of the teaching method employed in the
experimental group. Siyang (2018) suggests that using the first language as the
medium of instruction for mathematical problem-solving enables learners to better
understand the problem's question and the provided data. The study's findings
indicate that students who received the intervention demonstrated improvement in
their performance on the post-test compared to the pre-test.

Table 5 presents the comparison of pretest scores between the control
and experimental groups. The analysis indicates that there is no significant
difference between the scores of the pretests for the control group and
experimental group [t(df) = -0.88, p = .931] at the 5% significance level. This
suggests that there is sufficient evidence to conclude that there is no significant
variance between the pretest scores of the control and experimental groups. This
implies that there is no notable distinction in the students' performance between
the experimental and control groups before the treatment. Barwell (2009) suggests
that a strong foundation in the first language aids in conceptual understanding and
facilitates a smoother transition to the use of the second language in later
educational stages.
